title: Crowd-push calls bb_drop_ball unconditionally, stripping the ball from an unrelated carrier anywhere on the pitch
claim: When any player is pushed into the crowd, push_advance's PSH_CROWD branch calls bb_drop_ball(m) unconditionally. bb_drop_ball (engine/src/bb_match.c:95-101) operates on the GLOBAL m->ball.carrier, not on the pushed player. If the crowd-surfed defender is NOT the ball carrier but any other player on the pitch is, that carrier silently loses possession: their BB_PF_HAS_BALL flag is cleared, ball.carrier becomes BB_NO_PLAYER, and the ball is left BB_BALL_ON_GROUND under the carrier's square with no bounce, no turnover, and no way to re-pick it up until that square is vacated. Every subsequent decision in the drive is then computed from a corrupted ball state. Crowd pushes of non-carriers are routine in real play (sideline blocks), so this corrupts a meaningful fraction of training matches.
evidence: Trace: (1) push_legal emits BB_A_PUSH_SQUARE with arg=1 when all three push candidates are off-pitch (proc_block.c:561-569); push_apply sets PSH_CROWD (proc_block.c:586-589). (2) push_advance phase 2 then runs: `bool had_ball = (dp->flags & BB_PF_HAS_BALL) != 0; if (BB_TEAM_OF(def) == m->active_team) bb_turnover(m); bb_drop_ball(m);` (proc_block.c:424-426) — the drop is NOT gated on had_ball, only the THROW_IN push at 429-431 is. (3) bb_drop_ball at bb_match.c:95-101: `if (m->ball.carrier != BB_NO_PLAYER) { m->players[m->ball.carrier].flags &= ~BB_PF_HAS_BALL; m->ball.carrier = BB_NO_PLAYER; m->ball.state = BB_BALL_ON_GROUND; }` — acts on whoever the real carrier is. ball.x/y equals the carrier's square (maintained at bb_match.c:88-91, proc_move.c:174-184/262-263, proc_block.c:455-457/498-501), so the ball ends up loose underneath the still-standing carrier. Every other bb_drop_ball site in the engine guards on the affected player's HAS_BALL flag (proc_block.c:644-648 Saboteur, 779-787 direct injury, 962-965/1001-1004 foul send-off; proc_ttm.c:49, 112-116; proc_move.c:868-871), confirming the crowd-push site is an anomaly, not a convention. Fuzzing/ASan cannot catch it: carrier/flag/state stay mutually consistent — the state is legal, just wrong. Tests pass because golden traces encode the buggy behaviour.
fix: Gate the drop on the pushed player actually carrying: change proc_block.c:426 to `if (had_ball) bb_drop_ball(m);`. Then add a unit test: block a non-carrier into the crowd while a teammate elsewhere holds the ball, and assert the carrier still has BB_PF_HAS_BALL and ball.state == BB_BALL_HELD after the push resolves.

title: bb_apply re-enumerates the full legal action set on every step, duplicating work the caller already did
claim: Every bb_apply call re-runs the procedure's legal() enumerator into a fresh 16KB stack buffer (bb_action legal[BB_LEGAL_MAX], bb_match.c:287) and linearly scans it (bb_match.c:290-295) to validate the action — but on both hot callers (RL binding and fuzzer) the action is already guaranteed to be a member of a legal list enumerated from the exact same state, so the enumeration + scan is 100% redundant work that roughly doubles the per-step action-machinery cost.
evidence: Trace 1 (RL hot loop): puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h c_step:415-418 calls bbe_decode, which only ever returns elements of env->legal (bloodbowl.h:317-334); env->legal was filled by bbe_refresh_legal (bloodbowl.h:132-136, called at bloodbowl.h:439 at the end of the previous step) from the same unchanged engine state. bb_apply then re-enumerates via bb_legal_actions (bb_match.c:288) — the membership check can never fail on this path. Trace 2 (fuzz): engine/tests/fuzz_match.c:35-38 calls bb_legal_actions then bb_apply(legal[b%n]) — same state, enumeration runs twice per step across the ~140K-match campaigns. Cost is worst in setup: setup_legal (proc_match.c:278-312) iterates 16 players x ~195 own-half squares ≈ 3,120 inner iterations emitting ~12.5KB of actions per call, plus the validation scan averages ~1,500 4-byte compares — and per D1 an untrained policy burns the full 64-action setup budget x 2 teams x ~4-8 drives, making setup hundreds of decisions per early-training match, each paying the enumeration twice. Pass-interception windows re-run pass_legal -> interception_candidates -> ruler_candidates (proc_ball.c:186-211, 342-353), a 64-iteration double-precision line traversal, a second time per applied action.
fix: Add a trusted entry point, e.g. bb_status bb_apply_trusted(bb_match*, bb_action, bb_rng*) that skips the enumeration/membership check (or a variant accepting the caller's (legal, n) pair and doing only the O(n) membership scan), and use it from c_step (bloodbowl.h:418) and fuzz_match.c:38. Keep the checked bb_apply as the default for replay validation and tests. Expected win: removes one of the two full legal enumerations per step — ~10+ microseconds per setup decision and ~2x reduction of legal-enumeration cost across all decision types, for both training throughput and fuzz throughput.
votes: UPHELD: Claim survives full code-path verification. (1) bb_match.c:287-295 matches exactly: bb_apply declares bb_action legal[BB_LEGAL_MAX] (4096 x 4B = 16KB per bb_actions.h:97/114), re-enumerates via bb_legal_actions at line 288, and linearly scans 290-295. (2) RL-path redundancy proven, not just plausible: bbe_decode (puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:312-334) returns only members of env->legal on all three passes (pass 1/2 return env->legal[i], pass 3 returns env->legal[0]); env->legal is refreshed at bloodbowl.h:439/351 from the identical state, and nothing mutates env->match between refresh and bb_apply at bloodbowl.h:418 (binding.c only sets buffer pointers/kwargs; c_render/c_close are no-ops). All 13 legal enumerators take const bb_match* with no RNG parameter and contain no mutable statics, const-casts, or RNG use — enumeration is a pure function of state, so bb_apply's re-enumeration yields the identical list and the membership check can never fail on this path. (3) Fuzz path confirmed: fuzz_match.c:35-38 enumerates then applies legal[b%n] with no intervening mutation. (4) Cost evidence accurate: setup_legal (proc_match.c:278-312) is 16 slots x 195 squares = 3,120 iterations under D1's SETUP_ACTION_BUDGET=64 (proc_match.c:266); pass_legal (proc_ball.c:342-353) re-runs interception_candidates -> ruler_candidates (proc_ball.c:186-211, 64-step double-precision march) inside bb_apply. (5) Not pre-empted by design: D2 blesses 16KB stack per enumeration (buffer size), not double enumeration per applied action; bb_match.h:142 documents the checked contract, which the proposed fix preserves as default. Minor quibbles only: "roughly doubles per-step action-machinery cost" slightly overstates (caller-side decode+mask-fill are also O(n); enumeration itself exactly doubles, and the fix's "~2x legal-enumeration cost" framing is precise), and using bb_apply_trusted in fuzz_match.c would weaken the fuzzer's determinism oracle (the in-apply check is what would catch a nondeterministic enumerator there, per fuzz_match.c:39's abort-on-ERROR) — the fuzz half of the fix should keep the checked path or do the (legal,n)-membership variant. Neither quibble invalidates the finding; severity MEDIUM/perf is fair.

UPHELD: Claim survives the reachability attack — the redundant path executes on every step of both hot loops, and every evidence line checks out. (1) bb_apply (engine/src/bb_match.c:287-295) unconditionally re-enumerates into a 16KB stack buffer and scans; no NDEBUG/macro elision or trusted variant exists anywhere (grepped engine/ + puffer/; bb_apply at bb_match.h:143 is the only apply entry). (2) RL path: bbe_decode (puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:317-334) returns only env->legal elements; env->legal is refreshed at bloodbowl.h:439/:351 after the last match mutation, binding.c never touches the match otherwise, and all proc legal() enumerators are pure functions of const bb_match* with no RNG — so bb_apply's internal re-enumeration provably reproduces env->legal and the membership check cannot fail; the binding itself documents the failure branch as 'Should be unreachable' (bloodbowl.h:432-433). (3) Fuzz path: fuzz_match.c:34-38 enumerates then applies a member of that list against unchanged state — two enumerations per step, every step. (4) Cost evidence verified: setup_legal (proc_match.c:278-312) is exactly 16 slots x 13 cols x 15 rows = 3,120 inner iterations; ruler_candidates (proc_ball.c:186-211) is a 64-step double traversal re-run via pass_legal -> interception_candidates (proc_ball.c:342-353, 327-340) at the multi-candidate window (pass_advance proc_ball.c:295-298); D1's 64-action setup budget makes setup decisions plentiful in early training. (5) Not pre-blessed by DECISIONS.md: D2 blesses the 16KB stack per enumeration and the list's validation/fuzz/test purpose, not the doubled per-step CPU in the training hot loop; the proposed fix keeps the checked bb_apply as default for exactly those uses, and D24 (14.7K SPS measurement) shows env throughput is an active project concern. Only minor magnitude hedges found ('~10+ us per setup decision' may be ~2x optimistic; converting the fuzzer is optional since the membership check is provably self-consistent there and detects nothing the trusted variant wouldn't), neither of which refutes the finding. The '2x reduction in legal-enumeration cost' is arithmetically exact: 2 enumerations per applied action -> 1.

REFUTED: REFUTED on design-intent grounds. The claim's mechanical trace is accurate (bbe_decode at puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:312-335 only returns members of env->legal, so the membership check in bb_apply cannot fail on the RL or fuzz happy paths), but the "redundant work" framing and the proposed fix both contradict documented design, and the perf motivation is contradicted by the repo's own measured budget.

(1) The check IS the documented design, not incidental duplication. It is named in-code as an invariant ("Validate against the legal set (mask-soundness invariant)", engine/src/bb_match.c:286), it is the engine's documented API contract ("Applying an action not in bb_legal_actions sets BB_STATUS_ERROR", engine/include/bb/bb_match.h:142), and it is invariant #6 of validation layer 3 in the project's validation architecture (".claude/skills/bb-validation/SKILL.md:101-102: Mask-soundness — step() accepts action ⟺ bit set in legal mask. Metamorphic half: sampled masked-out actions are rejected without state mutation"), under the stated principle that "Validation is the product" (SKILL.md:8). The binding authors knowingly kept the redundancy: bloodbowl.h:432-434 says "Should be unreachable (decode snaps to legal); reset defensively" — they understood the check never fires on the happy path and deliberately built the defensive episode-reset on top of it. Knowing redundancy with a written rationale is a judgment call, per the review ground rules.

(2) The proposed fix breaks the documented invariant exactly where it is load-bearing. (a) Fuzzer: fuzz_match.c:4-6 explicitly lists "mask-soundness violations (BB_STATUS_ERROR after a legal apply)" as a target bug class — the checked bb_apply is part of the system under fuzz, and the in-apply re-enumeration is the only mechanism anywhere that verifies legal() enumerators are deterministic functions of const state; switching fuzz_match.c:38 to a trusted variant means the checked path that the Phase-4 FUMBBL replay differential (the authoritative validation layer, still pending) depends on would no longer be fuzzed at all — the cited "~140K fuzzed matches" guarantee is a guarantee about the checked entry point. (b) RL binding: CLAUDE.md:16 documents that masks exist ONLY on the CUDA backend and "The env must tolerate illegal sampled actions gracefully"; the tolerance chain is mask → decode snap-to-legal (bloodbowl.h:18-19) → engine validation (bb_match.c:286-299) → defensive reset (bloodbowl.h:432-434). With bb_apply_trusted at c_step, the defensive branch becomes dead code, and any future stale-env->legal bug (a forgotten bbe_refresh_legal) or decode regression — the prior deep review already fixed an unsound spatial discriminator in exactly this decode — feeds an unchecked action into vt->apply implementations that trust payloads blindly: setup_apply (engine/src/proc_match.c:314-322) passes a.arg/a.x/a.y straight into bb_place, which writes m->grid[x][y] and m->players[slot] with no bounds checks (bb_match.c:50-59). The check is a memory-safety boundary for unattended GPU runs, not just dead validation.

(3) The claimed perf win ("training throughput", MEDIUM) is refuted by the project's own measured, documented budget — measured WITH the double enumeration in place: STATUS.md:3 records 206K env-steps/sec/core (~2.1M/s aggregate) against a 500K target (4x over), and docs/policy-research.md:184 states "The BB engine (2.34 M engine-steps/s/core, STATUS.md) will not be the bottleneck" — the documented end-to-end ceiling is the policy forward at 0.5-1.5M SPS; policy-research.md:281 even says the engine "absorbs it without noticing" regarding extra per-decision steps. DECISIONS.md D2 separately shows the per-enumeration cost of this exact machinery (including the worst-case ~3000-action setup enumeration the claim leads with) was explicitly considered and accepted ("it's for replay validation/fuzzing/tests... fine"). Halving engine-side enumeration cost cannot improve training throughput when the engine already has ~4x headroom over the actual bottleneck; the same budget logic applies to fuzz throughput claims (the fuzzer's documented purpose includes fuzzing the checked path itself).

title: bb_hook_mods scans all 32 players' full skill bitsets on every D6 test for only 3 registered aura hooks
claim: The aura section of bb_hook_mods (bb_hooks.c:37-51) walks every other player slot (32-entry loop), and for each on-pitch player iterates every set skill bit via a bb_next_skill function call per bit plus a bb_hooks[sk].aura table load — on every test-modifier query of any kind. Only three skills in the entire codebase register an aura hook, and none of them applies to RUSH, PICKUP, or STANDUP tests, so for those kinds the whole ~20-player x ~5-bit scan (high hundreds of cycles) is provably dead work; for DODGE it is live for at most 2 of ~107 skills.
evidence: Registered aura hooks (exhaustive grep over engine/src): DISTURBING_PRESENCE (skills_core.c:71, returns 0 unless kind is PASS/CATCH), TITCHY (skills_devious_traits.c:39, DODGE only), PREHENSILE_TAIL (skills_mutation_passing.c:36, DODGE/JUMP only). Yet the dispatcher iterates unconditionally: bb_hooks.c:37 'for (int s = 0; s < BB_NUM_PLAYERS; s++)', bb_hooks.c:41-42 inner per-bit walk 'for (int sk = bb_next_skill(&q->skills, 0); ...)', bb_hooks.c:43 hook load per set bit. Call sites are the hottest test paths in the engine: every dodge (proc_move.c:751), pickup (proc_move.c:119, 196), rush (proc_move.c:151, 686, 738, 786), jump (proc_move.c:163, 698, 770), stand-up (proc_move.c:664), pass/catch/interception (proc_ball.c:146, 258, 361). With procgen squads averaging ~4 skills/player and ~20-22 players on pitch, each call performs ~100+ bb_next_skill invocations plus ~80-90 indexed loads into the 107-entry x 40B bb_hooks table — several hundred cycles per test, on a large fraction of decision steps.
fix: At registration time (the BB_SKILL_AURA constructor in bb_hooks.h:83-88), also (a) set the skill's bit in a global 'bb_skillset bb_aura_mask' and (b) record an 'aura_kinds' bitmask (by bb_test_kind) unioned into a global. In bb_hook_mods: if (!(aura_kinds_union & (1u << c->kind))) skip the whole 32-player loop (kills RUSH/PICKUP/STANDUP entirely); otherwise skip any player whose (skills.w[i] & bb_aura_mask.w[i]) intersection is zero across the 3 words before entering the bit-walk. This removes ~95%+ of the scan with 3 AND ops per player and zero behavior change.
votes: UPHELD: The claim survives a full code-path trace; every piece of cited evidence verifies against the source. (1) Dispatcher structure confirmed: bb_hooks.c:37-51 unconditionally loops all 32 slots (BB_NUM_PLAYERS=32, engine/include/bb/bb_types.h:19), and for each on-pitch player walks every set skill bit via bb_next_skill (bb_hooks.c:41-42) with a bb_hooks[sk].aura load per bit (bb_hooks.c:43); there is no kind-based early-out or aura-mask prefilter anywhere in the function. (2) Aura inventory exhaustive and correct: exactly three BB_SKILL_AURA registrations exist in the entire engine — DISTURBING_PRESENCE (skills_core.c:71, returns 0 unless kind==PASS||CATCH at :72), TITCHY (skills_devious_traits.c:39, returns 0 unless kind==DODGE at :40), PREHENSILE_TAIL (skills_mutation_passing.c:36, handles JUMP at :37 and DODGE at :55, else 0). I also checked for non-macro `.aura =` assignments (none) and the one suspect — TIMMM_BER, which bb_skills.h:23 notes modifies stand-up — is registered as an own-skill `mod` (skills_core.c:151), not an aura. Therefore for BB_TEST_RUSH/PICKUP/STANDUP/GENERIC every aura hook returns 0 and the whole 32-player scan is provably dead work, exactly as claimed; for DODGE only 2 hooks are live. (3) All 13 cited call sites verified with the exact kinds claimed: PICKUP proc_move.c:116-119 and :194-196, RUSH :148-151/:683-686/:735-738/:784-786, JUMP :158-163/:693-698, STANDUP :660-664, DODGE :747-751, plus CATCH proc_ball.c:141-146, PASS :253-258, interception-as-CATCH :358-361 (there is even an additional uncited site at proc_move.c:280). These fire on every step into/out of a tackle zone, every rush square, every pickup and MA<3 stand-up — genuinely the hottest test paths. (4) Magnitude arithmetic holds: bb_skill_hooks is 4 fn ptrs + 4 u8 + 1 i8 = 37B padded to 40B; ~20 on-pitch players x (~4 skills + 1 terminating bb_next_skill call) ≈ 100 bb_next_skill invocations + ~80 aura-pointer loads per query. (5) The proposed fix is behavior-preserving including the bb_skill_exercised coverage counters, since bb_cover only fires when v != 0 (bb_hooks.c:48) and all skipped hooks return 0. (6) Not pre-flagged design: D1-D24 in DECISIONS.md contain no decision about hook-dispatch cost, and bb_hooks.h:14 ("dispatcher scans both teams") describes the mechanism, not a documented tradeoff. Only trivial inaccuracies found, none load-bearing: BB_SKILL_COUNT is 108, not 107 (gen_skills.h:19-127); bb_next_skill lives in the same TU as bb_hook_mods (bb_hooks.c:8) so the compiler likely inlines the "function call per bit" at -O2 (the per-bit ctz/mask work remains either way); and the "high hundreds of cycles" figure is an unmeasured estimate — but the dead-work characterization and MEDIUM perf severity stand regardless of the exact constant. Also note skills_devious_traits.c:21-24 carries a stale comment claiming rush call sites do not consult bb_hook_mods — they do (proc_move.c:151 et al.), which further confirms the claim's call-site evidence over the stale comment.

(2) All claimed call sites check out with the claimed ctx kinds: BB_TEST_PICKUP at proc_move.c:116-122 and 194-199, BB_TEST_RUSH at 148-154 (the stale skills_devious_traits.c:22 comment saying rush is inline does NOT save the claim — rush ctxs do flow through bb_hook_mods), BB_TEST_JUMP at 158-165, BB_TEST_STANDUP at 660-664, pass/catch/interception at proc_ball.c:146/258/361. title: bb_cover() increments process-global shared counters from concurrently stepping OMP env threads — contended cache lines plus a data race
claim: bb_skill_exercised[BB_SKILL_COUNT] (defined bb_hooks.c:6, ~107 x 8B = ~14 cache lines) is a single process-global array, but PufferLib 4.0 steps all envs of a worker process concurrently under '#pragma omp parallel for ... c_step(&envs[i])'. Every bb_cover() call is therefore a non-atomic read-modify-write on a cache line shared by all worker threads: a C11 data race (UB; in practice lost increments that silently corrupt the layer-6 coverage metric) and, performance-wise, cache-line ping-pong on hot counters that makes each increment cost an exclusive-ownership transfer (~10-100x the 'one array increment; negligible' claim at bb_hooks.h:118) at training scale.
evidence: Threading: vendor/PufferLib/src/vecenv.h:292-294 — static_omp_threadmanager runs '#pragma omp parallel for schedule(static) num_threads(num_workers)' over 'c_step(&envs[i])', so envs in one process step on multiple threads sharing the binding TU's globals (bloodbowl.h amalgamates bb_hooks.c at line 35, one bb_skill_exercised per process). Hot increment sites: bb_hook_push_flags covers on every QUERY for every push-flag skill the player has (bb_hooks.c:87 'if (bb_hooks[sk].push_flags) bb_cover(sk);') and proc_block.c calls it up to 3x per block resolution (proc_block.c:390, 484, 535) — Stand Firm/Side Step/Fend counters sit adjacent in the array and get hammered on every block against such players; bb_hook_reroll covers per grant (bb_hooks.c:62); bb_hook_mods covers per non-zero modifier (bb_hooks.c:32, 48). Blocks and dodges are the most frequent resolved events in a match, so this is several contended shared-line RMWs per decision step across 8-64 OMP workers. Single-threaded ASan/UBSan runs and the fuzzer (fuzz_match.c is sequential) can never observe either the race or the contention.
fix: Make coverage instrumentation training-build-free or per-thread: simplest is '#ifdef BB_COVERAGE' around bb_cover's body (validation layer 6 runs are dedicated builds anyway); alternatives are per-env counters (add to a context struct merged at report time) or _Thread_local stripes summed on read. Any of these removes both the UB race and the cross-core cache traffic from the training hot loop.
votes: UPHELD: Claim survives full code-path trace. Verified end-to-end: (1) bb_skill_exercised is a plain non-atomic process-global (engine/src/bb_hooks.c:6, BB_SKILL_COUNT=108 per gen_skills.h:127, ~14 cache lines) and bb_cover (bb_hooks.h:120) is an unguarded plain ++ with no #ifdef anywhere; (2) the binding amalgamates bb_hooks.c (puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:35) so all envs in the trainer process share one array; (3) the CUDA trainer's only step path is static_vec_omp_step (vendor/PufferLib/src/bindings.cu:154) -> '#pragma omp parallel for schedule(static) num_threads(num_workers)' over c_step (vecenv.h:291-294), the binding is compiled with -fopenmp on Linux (build.sh:272-273; the D24 no-OpenMP workaround is Mac-only), and the repo's live config (puffer/config/bloodbowl.ini: num_buffers=1, num_threads=8) yields 8 OMP threads stepping 2048 envs concurrently in one process; (4) c_step (bloodbowl.h:409) -> bb_apply (:418) reaches bb_cover via bb_hook_push_flags (bb_hooks.c:87, called from proc_block.c:390/484/535), bb_hook_mods (bb_hooks.c:32/:48), and bb_hook_reroll (bb_hooks.c:61) — all cited lines exact. Concurrent unsynchronized uint64_t++ from 8 threads is a genuine C11 data race (UB, lost increments) plus real cache-line contention, and bb_skill_exercised is the only cross-thread mutable shared state in the binding; sequential fuzzer/ASan/coverage_report cannot observe it. Caveats (overstatements, not refutations): the 'corrupts the layer-6 coverage metric' impact is hollow — nothing in the training stack reads bb_skill_exercised; layer 6 is the standalone single-threaded tools/coverage_report.c binary (race-free) and uses counters only as a fired/not-fired boolean (:41), so training-time lost increments have no consumer. '8-64 workers' overstates the actual 8; perf impact is likely low single-digit percent of step time, so MEDIUM is at the generous end. The proposed fixes (#ifdef BB_COVERAGE / per-env counters / _Thread_local stripes) are all sound.

UPHELD: The core claim survives reachability attack — the full chain is verified concretely on the real training path: (1) binding.c:6 -> puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:35 amalgamates engine/src/bb_hooks.c into the single binding TU, so uint64_t bb_skill_exercised[BB_SKILL_COUNT] (bb_hooks.c:6) is one process-global array shared by all 2048 envs; (2) vendor/PufferLib/build.sh:273 compiles the env library with -fopenmp (Linux GPU training; D24's no-fopenmp path is Mac-practice only); (3) pufferlib.cu:2181 create_static_threads(hypers.num_threads,...) with puffer/config/bloodbowl.ini num_threads=8/num_buffers=1 gives num_workers=8 (vecenv.h:258), and vecenv.h:291-294 runs '#pragma omp parallel for num_threads(8)' over c_step(&envs[i]); (4) c_step (bloodbowl.h:418) -> bb_apply -> proc_block.c:390/484/535 bb_hook_push_flags -> bb_hooks.c:87 bb_cover, plus bb_hooks.c:32/48/61/75 — reroll grants (Dodge/Sure Feet/Sure Hands), activation gates, and push-flag covers fire constantly across 2048 concurrent procgen matches, so concurrent non-atomic RMWs on shared cache lines (8 counters per 64B line) occur every step batch. That is a real C11 data race (UB) plus genuine cross-core cache traffic in the training hot loop, and the 'negligible' comment at bb_hooks.h:117-118 indeed does not account for the OMP consumer. HOWEVER, two material corrections: (a) the claimed correctness harm is UNREACHABLE — the only reader of bb_skill_exercised in the repo is tools/coverage_report.c:41, a standalone strictly-sequential binary (make coverage) that plays its own matches in its own process and reads counters only as booleans; the training process's counters are never read by anything, so 'silently corrupt the layer-6 coverage metric' is false in every configuration; (b) magnitude is overstated — 8 OMP workers per config (not 8-64), and the few contended increments per c_step are a small fraction of step cost next to legal enumeration plus 2x832B obs encode and 2x454B mask fill (low single-digit percent at worst). Net: valid as a LOW/MEDIUM perf+hygiene finding (benign-in-practice UB plus minor cache ping-pong, fix via #ifdef BB_COVERAGE or per-thread stripes is apt), with the metric-corruption leg stripped.

REFUTED: The threading premise is real but both claimed harms are disproven, and the always-on instrumentation is a documented design call whose rationale survives. (1) The claim's only functional consequence — "lost increments that silently corrupt the layer-6 coverage metric" — is impossible: the layer-6 metric is computed solely by tools/coverage_report.c:10-46, a standalone single-threaded binary (`make coverage`) that plays its own 180 matches in-process and reads bb_skill_exercised at exit. Repo-wide grep confirms no other reader exists; neither puffer/bloodbowl/binding.c nor bloodbowl.c ever reads the counters, so training-process increments are write-only dead stores whose loss reaches no consumer. (2) This consumer model is itself documented design intent at engine/include/bb/bb_hooks.h:116-118: "Always-on (one array increment; negligible) — the report tool reads it after a corpus run" — i.e., counter accuracy is only meaningful in dedicated sequential corpus runs, exactly the situation that holds. Per review ground rules, documented judgment calls are by design and the new OMP information does not invalidate this one, because threaded-run counter accuracy is irrelevant by construction. (3) The perf half fails quantitatively: STATUS.md:3 records a measured 206K env-steps/sec/core (~4.9µs per c_step), while bb_cover fires only when a skill effect actually fires (non-zero mods at bb_hooks.c:32/48; push-flag covers only for the minority of roster players holding Stand Firm/Side Step/Fend per gen_teams.c; rerolls only on grants) — realistically well under 1 increment/step. Even at the claim's own worst-case ~100ns per contended RMW, overhead is ~0.2-2% of env-step time, and env stepping (8 threads per puffer/config/bloodbowl.ini [vec] num_threads=8, num_buffers=1 — not the claimed "8-64 workers") feeds a GPU-bound training loop. The header's "negligible" verdict holds at the measured numbers. What survives is only a formally-UB but functionally inert TSan-visible race on never-read counters — a hardening nicety, not a MEDIUM perf finding; as stated (corrupted validation metric + material training-scale cost), the finding is refuted.

title: bb_match_init indexes bb_team_defs[] with unvalidated team ids; replay loader feeds it file-derived values
claim: A replay file with an out-of-range "home"/"away" team id drives an out-of-bounds read of bb_team_defs[] (30 entries), producing a crash or garbage rosters; additionally the (uint8_t) cast stores a different team id in m->team_id than the id used to build the squad.
evidence: Trace: bb_replay.c:102-103 parses "home"/"away" with no range check (parse_int accepts any digits). test_golden.c:71 checks only `home < 0`, then test_golden.c:74 calls bb_match_init(&m, home, away). bb_match_init (bb_match.c:230-231) does `m->team_id[BB_HOME] = (uint8_t)home_team_id` (truncates) but passes the full int to default_squad, which does `const bb_team_def* td = &bb_team_defs[team_id]` (bb_match.c:199) — for id >= BB_TEAM_COUNT this reads past a 30-element const array, then dereferences garbage `positions`/`num_positions`; bb_match.c:236 also reads `bb_team_defs[home_team_id].apothecary`. bb_replay.h:5-7 declares this format the conduit for "FUMBBL-replay normalization output, and BC training data" — i.e. internet-derived files in Phase 4/6, not just committed goldens. For an id like 4294967299, (int) truncation silently selects team 3 while a corrupt id of 1000 segfaults. Other bb_match_init callers (fuzz_match.c:22-23, dist_dump.c:348, coverage_report.c:14) all mod by BB_TEAM_COUNT, confirming the invariant is expected but unenforced at the API boundary.
fix: In bb_match_init, validate `(unsigned)home_team_id < BB_TEAM_COUNT && (unsigned)away_team_id < BB_TEAM_COUNT`; on failure memset the match and set m->status = BB_STATUS_ERROR (callers already handle ERROR). Also make replay loaders (test_golden.c and the upcoming FUMBBL/BC harness) reject INIT records with out-of-range version/team ids.
votes: UPHELD: Claim survives full code-path verification. The complete chain is real: bb_replay.c:102-103 parses "home"/"away" via parse_int (lines 63-70) with no range check and an (int) cast that truncates int64 values (4294967299 -> 3, exactly as claimed); test_golden.c:71 gates only `home < 0` (away is never checked at all — even a negative away from a missing key passes), then line 74 calls bb_match_init; bb_match.c:230 stores `(uint8_t)home_team_id` (1000 -> 232) while lines 232-233 pass the full int to default_squad, which at bb_match.c:199 indexes `bb_team_defs[team_id]` unchecked — the array is exactly BB_TEAM_COUNT=30 entries (gen_teams.h:8-40,68, enum counted) — and bb_match.c:236 repeats the raw-int index for .apothecary. Refutation attempts all failed: (1) no validation exists anywhere between parser and index; (2) no documented precondition or assert makes this a by-design caller contract — bb_match.h:123-127 has no contract language and DECISIONS.md D1-D24 don't cover replay input validation; (3) the 140K-fuzz/ASan-clean record is not counter-evidence because fuzz_match.c:22-23, dist_dump.c:348, and coverage_report.c:12-16 all mod/bound by BB_TEAM_COUNT first, so out-of-range ids were structurally never exercised — confirming the unenforced-invariant point; (4) threat model is accurate: bb_replay.h:5-7 designates the format as the conduit for FUMBBL-normalization output and BC training data, and test_golden.c:9 states it is the same injection mechanism the FUMBBL differential harness will use (Phase 4/6). Only nit: `positions` is an inline array, not a pointer, so the failure is an OOB read at a garbage offset rather than a wild-pointer dereference — same outcome (crash or garbage rosters). Severity MEDIUM/hardening is correctly calibrated (current goldens are repo-committed; RL path uses bb_match_init_random and is unaffected). The proposed fix is coherent: BB_STATUS_ERROR exists (bb_match.h:72) and callers already branch on status.

REFUTED: The static observation is accurate (bb_match.c:199 indexes bb_team_defs[] with no bounds check; bb_replay.c:102-103 parses unvalidated; test_golden.c:71 checks only `home < 0`), but the claim fails the reachability lens on every path that exists. Exhaustive caller inventory: (1) fuzz_match.c:22-23, dist_dump.c:348, and coverage_report.c:12-16 all clamp via `% BB_TEAM_COUNT`; (2) test_match.c:42/129, test_rules_structure.c:282/1528, and gen_goldens.c:16-21/34 pass compile-time enum constants; (3) the actual training path (puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:351) never calls bb_match_init at all — it calls bb_match_init_random, whose ids come from `pg_pick(rng, BB_TEAM_COUNT)` (bb_procgen.c:128-129), so no training run can reach the flaw; (4) the sole file-derived caller, test_golden.c:74, reads only the hardcoded directory engine/tests/golden/ (test_golden.c:92,102) containing repo-committed traces generated by gen_goldens.c from its constant matchup table — all 8 committed init records verified in-range (ids 0-29 of 30). Reaching the OOB requires the developer to hand-corrupt a golden file in their own checkout, which is equivalent in trust level to editing the test source itself; there is no untrusted-input channel. The claim's escalation to 'internet-derived files in Phase 4/6' via bb_replay.h:5-7 is aspirational-comment evidence, not code: validation/normalize_replay.py emits a DIFFERENT schema ({"type":"meta"/"formation"/...} into validation/normalized/) with no bb_replay-format init record, explicitly disclaims engine lockstep ('it does not replay commands through the engine; the replay differential (validation layer 7) is the next milestone'), and no C code consumes its output — the only bb_replay_reader consumers in the entire tree are test_replay.c (in-process constants 3/7) and test_golden.c. The raylib viewer mentioned in the same header comment also does not exist (render/ contains no files; task pending). The claim's own fix text concedes this by targeting 'the upcoming FUMBBL/BC harness.' A vulnerability whose only trigger is either self-sabotage of committed test fixtures or code that has not been written yet is not reachable by any real match, training run, fuzz input (140K fuzzed matches structurally cannot hit it), or file-input channel today. Even granting the hand-corruption scenario, the blast radius is a unit-test binary reading const data OOB, not the GPU training run. Valid as a one-line defense-in-depth suggestion for the Phase 4 milestone; invalid as a present-tense MEDIUM finding.

UPHELD: Claim survives the design-intent attack. Every evidence line verified: bb_replay.c:101-104 parses home/away with no range check (parse_int accepts any digits; the (int) cast truncates int64 so 4294967299→3 as claimed); test_golden.c:71 checks only `home < 0 || end_home < 0` — away is never range- or even sign-checked — and :74 feeds both to bb_match_init; bb_match.c:230-231 stores (uint8_t)-truncated ids while passing the full int to default_squad, which indexes bb_team_defs[team_id] at bb_match.c:199 (array declared [BB_TEAM_COUNT]=30 at gen_teams.h:68) then dereferences td->positions at :205 — a genuine OOB read + garbage-pointer deref for id>=30 or <0; bb_match.c:236-237 adds OOB .apothecary reads. Design-intent gives no shelter: DECISIONS.md D1-D24 contain no judgment call about trusting replay input or team ids (they cover rules auto-policies and RL design only); CLAUDE.md conventions are silent; bb_match.h:124's "(codegen team ids)" is a parameter description, not a documented decision to skip validation. The 140K-fuzz/ASan-clean record cannot disprove it — fuzz_match.c:22-23 mods ids by BB_TEAM_COUNT before init, so out-of-range ids have zero sanitizer coverage; every other caller (dist_dump.c:348, coverage_report.c:14, enum-constant tests, gen_goldens.c:34) likewise enforces the invariant externally, confirming it is expected but unenforced at the API boundary. The proposed fix breaks no documented invariant: a range check rolls no dice (determinism preserved), and setting BB_STATUS_ERROR is already handled — bb_advance (bb_match.c:249) loops only on BB_STATUS_RUNNING, so test_golden.c would fail cleanly (-7) instead of hitting UB; the RL hot path uses bb_match_init_random (bloodbowl.h:351), whose ids come from pg_pick(rng, BB_TEAM_COUNT) (bb_procgen.c:128-129), so zero training impact. Severity MEDIUM/hardening is correctly calibrated: committed goldens are trusted today, but bb_replay.h:3-7 itself designates the format as the conduit for FUMBBL-replay normalization output and BC training data (Phase 4 pending / Phase 6 in progress), where a normalizer bug (e.g. an unmapped-roster sentinel like -1, which the away path doesn't even sign-check) flows straight into the OOB index.

title: Fuzzer never exercises bb_match_init_random — the exact init path every GPU training reset uses, including D21-deferred skills procgen hands out
claim: The ~140K-match fuzz corpus (random legal actions, ASan/UBSan, invariant aborts) only covers default-squad matches; the training run resets exclusively through procgen, whose random-advancement table grants skills (Multiple Block, Pile Driver) that DECISIONS.md D21 explicitly deferred on the premise of 'no default-roster carriers' — so the assurance story does not cover the squad/skill distribution actually being trained on.
evidence: fuzz_match.c:27 calls only `bb_match_init(&m, home, away)`. The env reset is `bb_match_init_random(&env->match, &env->procgen)` (puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:348, mirrored in vendor/PufferLib/ocean/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:348). Procgen advancement (bb_procgen.c:97-116) draws from bb_random_skill_table (gen_skills.c:116) whose strength row contains BB_SK_MULTIPLE_BLOCK and devious row BB_SK_PILE_DRIVER — `grep -rn 'MULTIPLE_BLOCK|PILE_DRIVER' engine/src/*.c` matches nothing outside the generated tables, i.e. zero hooks/procedures, so D21's 'no default-roster carriers' premise is invalidated every episode (skills are inert, silently). Procgen-only states also include 14-player squads, pre-CAS'd rosters, and auto-policy skills (Dump-Off proc_block.c:131-157, Saboteur, Trickster) on arbitrary roster combos. Existing procgen coverage is only test_match.c:160-186 — 12 matches steered by fx_pick_smart — versus 140K adversarial fuzz matches on the default-squad path; rare decision-window interactions are precisely what smart-policy picks avoid.
fix: Extend fuzz_match.c: use one input byte to select init mode, and for procgen mode seed a separate bb_rng from input bytes and call bb_match_init_random, keeping the same n<=0/ERROR/ball-invariant aborts. Separately, revisit D21: either implement Multiple Block/Pile Driver decision windows or remove them from bb_random_skill_table so procgen matches the implemented ruleset.

title: Animal Savagery lash-out KNOCKDOWN is deferred until after the entire declared action; can fire on an off-pitch player (location overwrite, CAS resurrection) or be silently discarded by a touchdown
claim: activation_apply pushes the lash-out KNOCKDOWN for the team-mate BEFORE pushing the MOVE frame for the declared action, so the LIFO stack resolves the whole action first and the team-mate's knockdown (stance change, ball drop/turnover, armour+injury dice) only after the action completes. During the action the supposedly-downed mate is still Standing: he exerts TZ/assists, blocks Wrestle/Block normally (proc_block.c:288-306 read live stance), and can legally receive a hand-off — after which the deferred knockdown bounces the ball and latches a turnover. Worse, if the mate is removed from the pitch during the action (chain-pushed into the crowd: crowd injury can put him in KO/CAS/RESERVES), the deferred knockdown still runs: knockdown_advance has no location guard, sets stance=PRONE on an off-pitch player and rolls ARMOUR→INJURY for him; the injury outcome paths use `else p->location = BB_LOC_KO` (proc_block.c:828), so a player already in the CAS box is overwritten to KO, then END_DRIVE's KO recovery (proc_match.c:694-696, d6>=4) returns him to RESERVES — a casualty resurrects mid-match. If instead the active player scores a TD during the action, touchdown_advance truncates the stack to the MATCH frame (proc_match.c:667-674), silently discarding the pending KNOCKDOWN so the lash-out never happens at all.
evidence: proc_turn.c:334-360: on a failed Animal Savagery roll, `bb_knockdown2(m, mate, BB_KD_TTM_LANDING, 0, f->a);` (line 355) pushes KNOCKDOWN; execution then falls through to `bb_push(m, BB_PROC_MOVE, f->a, a.arg, 0, 0);` (line 385), leaving the stack [...ACTIVATION, KNOCKDOWN(mate), MOVE]. bb_advance (bb_match.c:247-271) always runs the top frame, so MOVE — the full Move/Block/Blitz with all its decisions and dice — resolves to completion before KNOCKDOWN. knockdown_advance (proc_block.c:627-688) never checks `p->location == BB_LOC_ON_PITCH`: it writes `p->stance = BB_STANCE_PRONE` (line 653) and unconditionally pushes ARMOUR (line 687); injury_advance's KO branch does `if (p->location == BB_LOC_ON_PITCH) bb_remove_from_pitch(m, slot, BB_LOC_KO); else p->location = BB_LOC_KO;` (proc_block.c:827-828), which overwrites CAS/RESERVES locations. end_drive_advance (proc_match.c:694-696) then rolls KO'd players back to RESERVES on 4+. touchdown_advance (proc_match.c:667-674) sets `m->stack_top = i + 1` at the MATCH frame, dropping any pending KNOCKDOWN frame. The comment at proc_turn.c:354 ('Knocked down; turnover only if the mate held the ball') shows the knockdown was intended to take effect at declaration time, not after the action.
fix: In activation_apply, push the KNOCKDOWN AFTER pushing the MOVE frame (so it sits on top and resolves first; a resulting turnover then correctly aborts the action via MOVE's m->turnover check at proc_move.c:375), or resolve the lash-out through a dedicated ACTIVATION phase before declaring the action. As hardening, make knockdown_advance a no-op when `m->players[f.a].location != BB_LOC_ON_PITCH`.

Stack is therefore [..., ACTIVATION, KNOCKDOWN(mate), MOVE]: the entire declared action resolves before the lash-out. The project's OWN rules reference contradicts this ordering: .claude/skills/bb-rules/reference/skills-and-traits.md:140 says the player 'may continue activation AFTER lashing out (FAQ)' — lash-out resolves first. title: Pro re-roll is unreachable whenever it is the only available re-roll source: the TEST decision window only opens for team/skill re-rolls
claim: test_advance's reroll-window gate checks only team_rr and skill_rr; Pro is not considered. A player with Pro whose team has no usable team re-roll (rerolls exhausted, or the test occurs outside their team turn — e.g. a catch/interception during the opponent's turn) and no matching skill re-roll has his failed test popped immediately with no decision, even though test_legal explicitly enumerates BB_RR_PRO as a legal choice whenever the window does open. The two handlers are inconsistent about the same state: Pro is offered as an action in states the engine pauses at, but the engine never pauses for Pro alone. Net effect: Pro silently does nothing exactly when it matters most (late-half with 0 team re-rolls — a very common situation), measurably changing results for every Pro carrier.
evidence: proc_test.c:50-56: `bool team_rr = team_reroll_available(m, team) && !(f->data & TF_TEAM_USED); bool skill_rr = !(f->data & TF_SKILL_USED) && bb_skill_reroll_for(m, slot, f->b) >= 0; if (team_rr || skill_rr) { ... bb_need_decision ... }` — no Pro term; otherwise `bb_pop(m); m->ret = 0|...` (lines 57-58) ends the test as failed. Yet test_legal (proc_test.c:93-97) offers Pro under the independent condition `!(f->data & TF_TEAM_USED) && bb_has_skill(..., BB_SK_PRO) && !(flags & BB_PF_USED_SKILL_B)`, and test_apply (proc_test.c:119-128) fully implements the Pro path. team_reroll_available (proc_test.c:29-33) requires `m->rerolls[team] > 0 && m->active_team == team`, so the window provably never opens for a Pro-only player when rerolls==0 or when it is not their team turn. No TODO/deferral note exists for Pro anywhere in proc_test.c.
fix: Add a Pro term to the gate in test_advance phase 0: `bool pro_rr = !(f->data & TF_TEAM_USED) && bb_has_skill(&m->players[slot].skills, BB_SK_PRO) && !(m->players[slot].flags & BB_PF_USED_SKILL_B);` and open the window on `team_rr || skill_rr || pro_rr`. Add a unit test: Pro player, 0 team re-rolls, no skill re-roll, failed test → expect a decision offering BB_RR_PRO.

(2) Neither gate term can cover Pro: team_rr requires rerolls[team]>0 && active_team==team (proc_test.c:29-33); skill_rr calls bb_skill_reroll_for → bb_hook_reroll (bb_skills.c:5-10, bb_hooks.c:55-66) which scans reroll_kinds, and the ONLY BB_SKILL_REROLL registrations in the codebase are skills_core.c:13-22 (DODGE, SURE_FEET, SURE_HANDS, PASS, CATCH) — BB_SK_PRO registers no hook. title: Leader's per-half bonus re-roll is wiped by the Brilliant Coaching expiry clamp at the end of the FIRST drive of each half
claim: PREGAME grants the Leader bonus by incrementing m->rerolls[t] WITHOUT touching m->rerolls_start[t]. END_DRIVE then clamps `rerolls` back down to `rerolls_start` after EVERY drive (intended to expire the Brilliant Coaching drive-scoped bonus). The clamp cannot distinguish the two bonuses, so an unused Leader re-roll is silently deleted at the end of the first drive of the half — in any half containing more than one drive (i.e. any half with a TD before the final turn, which is most halves), the team loses its Leader re-roll for all subsequent drives. The code's own comments declare per-half semantics in two places, and the half-time path re-adds it, so this is an internal inconsistency provable without the rulebook.

title: BB_PF_DISTRACTED is never cleared for players without an activation-gate negatrait: Hypnotic Gaze victims stay Distracted for the rest of the drive
claim: The only mid-drive code path that clears BB_PF_DISTRACTED is inside activation_advance's negatrait-gate block, which executes only when bb_hook_activation_gate returns a registered gate skill (Bone Head, Really Stupid, Unchannelled Fury, Take Root). Players who become Distracted WITHOUT carrying one of those traits — any opponent hit by Hypnotic Gaze (proc_move.c:899), or an Animal Savagery player who lashes out with no adjacent mate (proc_turn.c:357) — can never have the flag cleared until end_drive's blanket `p->flags = 0`. bb_types.h documents the flag as 'BB2025 Distracted status (until end of next activation)', and turn_start's clear mask omits it deliberately for gate-carriers, but for gate-less players the result is a drive-long debuff: no tackle zone (bb_exerts_tz), cannot catch (bb_can_catch), cannot assist (bb_can_assist), Block/Wrestle disabled in block resolution, exempted from Secure-the-Ball denial — persisting across many turns instead of one activation. Wrong results in every match featuring Hypnotic Gaze (vampire-family rosters in the 30-team set) and in Animal Savagery no-mate cases.
evidence: Set sites: proc_move.c:899 (`m->players[target].flags |= BB_PF_DISTRACTED;` — Gaze, arbitrary victim), proc_turn.c:190 (gate failure, LOSE_ACT_AND_TZ) and proc_turn.c:357 (savagery, no mate). Clear sites: only proc_turn.c:197-200 — `if (gk != BB_GATE_ROOTED) { m->players[slot].flags &= ~BB_PF_DISTRACTED; }` — which is nested inside `if (bb_hook_activation_gate(m, slot, &target, &gk) >= 0 && ...)` (proc_turn.c:180-181); bb_hook_activation_gate (bb_hooks.c:68-80) returns -1 unless the player has a registered gate (skills_core.c:88-129 registers only BONE_HEAD/REALLY_STUPID/UNCHANNELLED_FURY/TAKE_ROOT — Animal Savagery and Hypnotic Gaze register none). turn_start's flag-clear mask (proc_turn.c:21-22) excludes BB_PF_DISTRACTED; the only other clear is end_drive_advance's `p->flags = 0` (proc_match.c:697). The activation wrap-up branch (proc_turn.c:208-211) sets USED but does not clear DISTRACTED. Documented intent: bb_types.h:70 `BB_PF_DISTRACTED = 1 << 2, // BB2025 Distracted status (until end of next activation)`.

Add unit tests: gazed player activates (Move, no gate) → flag gone afterwards; gazed player exerting no TZ regains it next turn.

title: Setup legality (~3120 iterations, ~3000 actions) is enumerated TWICE per RL step because bb_apply re-derives the legal set the binding already holds
claim: Every SETUP decision step pays two full setup_legal enumerations plus an O(n) linear validation scan, even though the action being applied was returned verbatim from the binding's already-materialized legal list. Under untrained policies (which per D1 burn the full 64-action budget per team per drive), setup steps cost ~50-100x a normal MOVE step and dominate engine-side throughput early in training; the bb_apply re-enumeration half of that cost is pure duplicate work. The same duplication applies to every decision type (kickoff_legal phase 0 emits 195 actions at proc_match.c:575-578; move/test legal sets are smaller but also enumerated twice).
evidence: Trace: (1) setup_legal at proc_match.c:295-310 loops 16 slots x 195 own-half squares = 3120 iterations, emitting up to ~3000 4-byte actions (~12KB of stores) per call. (2) The binding caches this list: bloodbowl.h:127 `bb_action legal[BB_LEGAL_MAX]` filled by bbe_refresh_legal (bloodbowl.h:132-136) at the end of every c_step. (3) bbe_decode (bloodbowl.h:312-335) returns elements of env->legal verbatim on every return path, so the action passed to bb_apply is legal by construction. (4) bb_apply (bb_match.c:287-299) nevertheless declares a fresh `bb_action legal[BB_LEGAL_MAX]` (16KB stack), calls bb_legal_actions — re-running setup_legal's 3120-iteration loop — then linear-scans with bb_action_eq (avg ~1500 4-byte compares for setup). Per match with ~300-500 budget-driven setup steps that is ~1.5-2.3M redundant loop iterations plus ~18MB of redundant action stores, on top of the decode (up to 3x3000 scan, bloodbowl.h:317-334) and mask-fill (3000 iterations, bloodbowl.h:304-309) passes that are inherent to the design. Note the premise in bb_actions.h:112-113 and D2 ('the RL action heads never materialize this list') is stale — the binding materializes and iterates it every step.
fix: Add a trusted entry point for the binding: either bb_apply_unchecked(m, a, rng) that skips the validate-by-enumeration block (sound because bbe_decode only ever returns env->legal[i]), or an index-based bb_apply_idx(m, env->legal, i, rng). Keep the existing checked bb_apply for fuzzing/replay/tests. This removes one full legal() enumeration + the O(n) bb_action_eq scan from every training step; for setup steps that is ~35-40% of the engine-side step cost, and it restores the documented D2 invariant that the 4096-entry list is only for validation/tests.

(2) The cost is already measured and documented as acceptable with 4x headroom: STATUS.md:3 reports 206K env-steps/sec/core (~2.1M/s aggregate vs a 500K target), measured by puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.c which per its own header (lines 1-7) plays random-policy matches "through the exact c_reset/c_step path training uses" — i.e., the benchmark already includes the untrained-policy setup-budget worst case (D1's 64-action budget) and the double enumeration the claim costs out. docs/policy-research.md:178-184 then draws the explicit design conclusion: end-to-end training is GPU-forward-bound at 0.5-1.5M SPS and "The BB engine (2.34 M engine-steps/s/core, STATUS.md) will not be the bottleneck." By the claim's own arithmetic (35-40% of setup-step engine cost, setup steps a minority of decisions, env a minority of wall time — 14.7K SPS practice-run train loop vs 206K/core env per D24/STATUS.md), the fix buys low-single-digit percent end-to-end at best, against documented 4x headroom — the same trade shape the project explicitly documented declining in D23 ("Documented, not fixed") for another transient untrained-policy artifact. (3) The claim's "stale D2 premise" charge misreads D2/bb_actions.h:110-113: "the factored RL action heads never materialize this list" means there is no 4096-way action head — still true, the heads are {30,33,391} (bloodbowl.h:63-65). The binding's env->legal cache for mask-fill/decode is the separately documented D22/D23/STATUS.md Phase-5 design, not a drift from D2. (4) The fix's stated benefit ("restores the documented D2 invariant that the list is only for validation/tests") is incoherent: with bb_apply_unchecked the binding still materializes and iterates the full list every step for bbe_refresh_legal, bbe_fill_mask (bloodbowl.h:295-313) and bbe_decode — passes the claim itself concedes are "inherent to the design." Net: a real but deliberately accepted duplication, blessed by measured-and-documented throughput budgets, whose removal on the training path would weaken a named validation-architecture invariant for negligible end-to-end gain.

title: bb_cover() global counters: non-atomic shared writes from every legality/test hot path race and ping-pong cache lines across OpenMP env workers
claim: The skill-coverage instrumentation increments a single process-global array (bb_skill_exercised) from the hottest engine paths. On the real CUDA training path, PufferLib steps all envs of a buffer in one process under `#pragma omp parallel for`, so dozens of threads do non-atomic read-modify-writes to the same ~1KB array: a C11 data race (UB, never exercised by the single-threaded ASan/UBSan fuzzing) and a cache-coherence hotspot — common-skill counters (Dodge, Block, Loner, Sure Feet) cluster on a few cache lines that every worker invalidates several times per decision, costing coherence misses in the tightest loops.
evidence: Write sites on the per-step path: a failed test fires bb_skill_reroll_for at proc_test.c:51 (test_advance) -> bb_hook_reroll (bb_hooks.c:55-66) which calls bb_cover(sk) at bb_hooks.c:62 merely for FINDING a reroll skill; test_legal repeats the query at proc_test.c:98 (and again inside bb_apply's revalidation, bb_match.c:288), so one failed dodge by a Dodge player = 3 shared-counter RMWs; bb_hook_mods covers per non-zero modifier per test (bb_hooks.c:32,48), bb_hook_push_flags per push (bb_hooks.c:87), plus direct calls at proc_test.c:121 and proc_turn.c:330,338,367. bb_cover is `bb_skill_exercised[skill_id]++` on a plain extern uint64_t array (bb_hooks.h:119-120). Threading: vendor/PufferLib/src/vecenv.h:291-294 runs `#pragma omp parallel for ... for (i...) c_step(&envs[i]);` in-process. The header's 'Always-on (one array increment; negligible)' note (bb_hooks.h:117-118) was a single-thread judgment; the multi-thread race/contention on the OMP training path is undocumented. Side effect: bb_legal_actions (a const-qualified query) mutates the counters, and bb_hook_reroll covers on query rather than on use, so coverage counts are inflated by enumeration anyway.
fix: Gate the instrumentation at compile time: `#ifdef BB_COVERAGE` in bb_cover (coverage_report builds define it; the binding build does not) — zero hot-path cost and no race. If always-on counts are wanted during training, make the array per-env (move into bb_match or a TLS shard aggregated at episode end). Independently, move the bb_cover in bb_hook_reroll to the consumption site (test_apply skill-reroll branch) so coverage counts fires, not queries.

title: ktm_used is never reset: first Kick Team-Mate permanently disables KTM for both teams for the rest of the match
claim: turn_start() (proc_turn.c:9-19) resets blitz_used, pass_used, handoff_used, foul_used, ttm_used, secure_used and turnover, but NOT ktm_used. ktm_used is set on KTM declaration (proc_turn.c:382) and gates KTM legality (proc_turn.c:295), and the only thing that ever zeroes it is the memset in bb_match_init/bb_match_init_random. Since it is also a single field shared by both teams, the first KTM declaration by either coach locks out Kick Team-Mate for BOTH teams for the remainder of the match, contradicting the header's own contract.
evidence: grep over the whole repo shows exactly three references: bb_match.h:101 (declares it as 'Kick Team-mate: its own per-turn budget'), proc_turn.c:295 (legality gate `!m->ktm_used`), proc_turn.c:382 (`case BB_ACT_KTM: m->ktm_used = 1`). No reset exists in turn_start (proc_turn.c:9-19, which resets every sibling budget), in end_drive_advance (proc_match.c:681-708), or anywhere else. KTM is carried by a stock roster (gen_teams.c:212, BB_SK_KICK_TEAM_MATE) and the Ogre-vs-Snotling golden matchup (DECISIONS D6), so it is reachable in default/procgen training matches. The fuzzer cannot catch this: fuzz_match.c only asserts crash/mask-soundness/ball invariants, never per-turn budget semantics.
fix: Add `m->ktm_used = 0;` to turn_start() alongside `m->ttm_used = 0;` (proc_turn.c:16), and add a unit test that declares KTM on turn N and asserts BB_ACT_KTM is legal again for the same team on turn N+1 and for the opponent in between.

title: Team re-rolls are offered during kickoff resolution because MATCH sets active_team to the receiver before pushing KICKOFF
claim: team_reroll_available() (proc_test.c:29-33) gates team re-rolls only on `m->rerolls[team] > 0 && m->active_team == team`. MATCH phase 2 assigns `m->active_team = 1 - m->kicking_team` BEFORE pushing KICKOFF (proc_match.c:38-40), so during the whole kickoff resolution (landing catch via kickoff_land → BB_PROC_CATCH, proc_match.c:514-516 → TEST at proc_ball.c:149, plus bounce catches and High Kick catches) the receiving coach is offered a team re-roll on a failed catch. BB2025 restricts team re-rolls to 'when the team is active' / 'during their turn' — the kick-off sequence precedes any team turn (no turn marker has moved), and FUMBBL/FFB never offer a re-roll there. This shifts kickoff-reception odds in every match and will produce a guaranteed divergence in the Phase 4 FUMBBL replay differential on every failed kickoff catch.
evidence: Code path: proc_match.c:39 `m->active_team = (uint8_t)(1 - m->kicking_team);` then line 40 pushes BB_PROC_KICKOFF. kickoff_land (proc_match.c:505-520) pushes BB_PROC_CATCH for the receiving player; catch_advance pushes BB_PROC_TEST (proc_ball.c:149); on a failed die, test_advance consults team_reroll_available (proc_test.c:50) which returns true for the receiver. Rules mirror (docs/vendor/bloodbowlbase/.../bb2025/core_rules/rules_and_regulations): 'Team Re-rolls can only be used when the team is active' and 'A Coach may use as many Team Re-rolls as they want during their turn'; the engine already has the exact predicate needed (`bb_in_kickoff`, proc_match.c:356-361) and uses it for the analogous throw-in suppression. Not documented as a deliberate approximation in DECISIONS.md D1-D24.
fix: Add `&& !bb_in_kickoff(m)` to team_reroll_available (proc_test.c:32), or defer the active_team assignment until MATCH phase 3 pushes the first TEAM_TURN. Add a unit test scripting a failed kickoff catch with rerolls available and asserting no reroll decision window opens.

title: BB_A_JUMP clobbers the stashed TTM/KTM team-mate slot in MOVE frame bits 9-13 — engine then throws player slot 0, potentially an off-pitch player (grid corruption)
claim: During a TTM/KTM activation the picked team-mate slot is stashed in MOVE-frame data bits 9-13 (BB_A_SPECIAL_TARGET arg 7). The thrower may still legally move, and a BB_A_JUMP overwrites those same bits with the pending rush count (left at 0 after the jump resolves). The subsequent BB_A_TTM_TARGET then reads mate=0 and pushes BB_PROC_TTM for player slot 0 regardless of team, adjacency, Right Stuff, or location. If players[0] is off-pitch, ttm_advance's manual grid clear uses its stale coordinates and erases an unrelated on-pitch player's grid entry, then bb_place teleports the reserve player onto the pitch.
evidence: Stash: proc_move.c:825-828 `if (a.arg == 7) { f->data |= MV_BLOCK_DONE; f->data = ((f->data & ~(31u << 9)) | ((uint16_t)target << 9)); }`. Read: proc_move.c:815-818 `int mate = (f->data >> 9) & 31; ... bb_push(m, BB_PROC_TTM, mate, slot, a.x, a.y);`. Clobber: proc_move.c:680 in BB_A_JUMP `f->data = ((f->data & ~(31u << 9)) | ((uint16_t)rushes_needed << 9));` (and the decrement loop at proc_move.c:144-145 leaves the field 0). Interleaving is legal: move_legal sets may_move for kind==BB_ACT_TTM/KTM (proc_move.c:421), offers BB_A_JUMP whenever budget>=2 and an adjacent non-standing player exists (proc_move.c:439-462) — the just-picked PRONE Right Stuff mate itself qualifies as jumpable — while TTM_TARGET squares are offered simultaneously once MV_BLOCK_DONE is set (proc_move.c:523-534). Consequence in proc_ttm.c: ttm_advance line 84 `m->grid[ox][oy] = 0` with ox,oy = players[0].x/y, which for a RESERVES/KO/CAS player are stale and may currently hold a different on-pitch player; then bb_place(mate,...) at proc_ttm.c:157 puts the off-pitch player on the board. The 140K-match fuzzer drives random LEGAL actions, so this sequence (declare TTM, pick mate, jump, throw) is reachable.
fix: Don't share the bit field: either stop offering BB_A_JUMP (and any bits-9-13 writer) once MV_BLOCK_DONE is set for kind TTM/KTM, or re-validate the mate at BB_A_TTM_TARGET time (same team, adjacent, Right Stuff, ON_PITCH; set BB_STATUS_ERROR otherwise). Add a regression test: TTM declare -> pick mate -> jump -> TTM target; assert the originally picked mate is the one thrown and grid stays consistent.

title: POW/Stumble knockdowns drop the causer: Mighty Blow, Claws, Dirty Player, Saboteur and all causer-side armour/injury hooks never fire on the main block-knockdown path
claim: push_advance phase 5 resolves a POW (or Stumble-without-Dodge) with bb_knockdown(m, def, BB_KD_BLOCK, 0) — the no-causer variant — so the ARMOUR/INJURY chain sees causer=-1 and every causer-gated effect is skipped. Since an MB/Claws attacker virtually always selects POW/Stumble (Both Down or Attacker Down are the only faces that still carry the causer), Mighty Blow and Claws are effectively inert in normal play, despite D16 documenting a detailed MB auto-policy and armour_advance containing dedicated (now unreachable) MB/Claws code. This systematically skews armour-break and injury rates for every bash roster (Minotaur, Rat Ogre, Trolls, Mummies, Werewolves all carry MB/Claws in gen_teams.c).
evidence: proc_block.c:517-518 `if (data & PSH_POW) { bb_knockdown(m, def, BB_KD_BLOCK, 0); }` — bb_knockdown (bb_match.c:117-119) pushes KNOCKDOWN with y=0, i.e. causer+1=0. knockdown_advance forwards it: proc_block.c:687 `bb_push(m, BB_PROC_ARMOUR, f.a, 0, f.x, f.y)` with f.y=0; armour_advance proc_block.c:697 `int causer = (int)f.y - 1;` = -1, so the Saboteur gate (proc_block.c:640 requires `(int)f.y - 1 >= 0`), MB-on-armour (proc_block.c:737-745, requires causer>=0), Claws 8+ (proc_block.c:747-748), DP (proc_block.c:731-732) and chain_mod's causer side (bb_hooks.c: `if (sides[i] < 0) continue;`) are all skipped; same for injury (proc_block.c:790-791). The only knockdown2 calls carrying a block causer are ATTACKER_DOWN (proc_block.c:270) and BOTH_DOWN (proc_block.c:309-310). grep confirms MIGHTY_BLOW appears nowhere in engine/tests except an elite-skill count (test_gen_data.c:29), so no test exercises the POW path with MB — consistent with 230 green tests coexisting with this bug.
fix: Snapshot the attacker before the pop in push phase 5 (int att = f->a; — valid because PSH_POW only exists on the root, non-chain PUSH frame) and call bb_knockdown2(m, def, BB_KD_BLOCK, 0, att). Add unit tests for MB and Claws through a chosen POW die (armour +1 conversion and Claws 8+ auto-break).

title: Frenzy second block is dead wiring: BLK_FRENZY_2ND is read and propagated but never set anywhere, and the PUSH-side latch (bit 7) is never read
claim: The code is scaffolded for Frenzy's mandatory second block — BLK_FRENZY_2ND is checked in resolve_face for PUSH_1/2, STUMBLE and POW and forwarded to the PUSH frame as bit 7 ("PSH_FRENZY_DONE") — but no code path ever sets BLK_FRENZY_2ND and nothing ever reads PUSH bit 7, and no second BLOCK is ever pushed after a frenzy follow-up. Frenzy currently only forces the follow-up (push phase 3). The wiring is provably dead: even if something set bit 15 before the dice, block_advance phase 0 wipes it at the data rebuild. Frenzy carriers are widespread in default rosters (Witch Elf, Troll Slayer, Minotaur, Rat Ogre, Werewolf, Bloodseeker...), so block outcomes are materially wrong in most matches.
evidence: Definition proc_block.c:50; reads at proc_block.c:316, 328, 338 (`uint16_t f2 = f->data & BLK_FRENZY_2ND;` then `if (f2) bb_top(m)->data |= 1 << 7;`). Repo-wide grep shows no write of BLK_FRENZY_2ND and no reader of PUSH data bit 7 (the PSH_* enum at proc_block.c:39-40 stops at bit 6); grep for "frenzy" in proc_turn.c, proc_match.c, skills_*.c and bb_skills.c returns nothing. Additionally block_advance phase 0 rebuilds frame data as `f->data = ((f->data & BLK_IS_BLITZ) | ((nd-1)<<9) | ...)` (proc_block.c:188), erasing bit 15 before any die is chosen, so the flag could never survive to resolve_face even if set at declaration. push_advance phase 3 (proc_block.c:492-505) performs only the forced follow-up and goes to phase 5 — no second BLOCK push exists. D3's Phase-2 deferral of Frenzy was supposed to land in Phase 3 (task list marks Phase 3 'full ruleset' complete), so this is an unfinished feature, not a documented policy.
fix: Either implement it (after a non-POW frenzy push where the defender is still standing and the attacker followed up adjacent, push a second BLOCK with BLK_FRENZY_2ND set at creation — after the data rebuild point — and use the PSH bit-7 latch to stop a third) or delete the dead flags so the FUMBBL differential flags the gap explicitly. Add a spec test for the double-block sequence.

title: Inaccurate pass landing on an empty square never takes its final bounce, contradicting the file's own spec comment
claim: Per BB2025 (and this file's own header, lines 16-21: 'empty square after a SCATTER -> one final bounce' / data bit0 doc 'an empty landing square bounces once'), a ball that scatters from an inaccurate pass and lands in an unoccupied square must bounce once more. scatter_advance instead lets it come to rest immediately for scatter and bounce alike (line 59), so every inaccurate pass landing on an empty square ends one D8 hop short. This shifts loose-ball positions in essentially every inaccurate pass — wrong results in realistic play, and a guaranteed dice/position divergence against the FUMBBL/FFB differential (validation layer 7) and recorded replays.
evidence: Trace: pass_advance phase 1 inaccurate latch (proc_ball.c:285) -> pass_resolve_flight (lines 217-224): bb_push(SCATTER, hops=3, target) with data|=1 (pass-flight). scatter_advance (lines 23-60): is_bounce=false (line 27); after the 3 hops, if the final square has a standing catcher -> CATCH mod 0 (line 50, correct), a non-catcher occupant -> bounce (lines 53-58, correct), but an EMPTY square hits line 59: bb_ball_to(m,x,y) '// comes to rest (scatter and bounce alike)' — no final bounce, directly contradicting the procedure's documented contract at lines 19-21. The is_bounce flag is consulted only for the catch modifier, never for the landing rule. D5's documented pass approximations (Euclidean range bands, natural-1 fold, deferred interception) do not cover this, and interception has since been implemented — the header comment shows the intended behavior, so this is an implementation slip, not a judgment call.
fix: In scatter_advance, when !is_bounce and the post-hop square is empty, push one final bounce instead of resting: bb_ball_to(m, x, y); bb_push(m, BB_PROC_SCATTER, 0, 1, (uint8_t)x, (uint8_t)y); (the pushed frame has data=0 = bounce, giving the correct -1 catch mod if it then hits a player). Add a unit test: inaccurate pass to an empty area consumes exactly 4 D8s (3 scatter + 1 bounce).
votes:

Empirical proof of reachability inside the repo: two GREEN tests execute exactly this path with scripted dice — test_rules_ball.c:105-121 (ball_pass_quick_short_boundary case b, script {3,5,5,5} = 1 PA + 3 scatter, asserts rest at (10,10)) and test_rules_ball.c:170-186 (long-bomb inaccurate, script {5,5,5,5}, rest at (18,7)). Since the 230-test suite is green, the engine demonstrably takes this path today. The rules premise also holds against the project's authoritative spec source: the BB2025 mirror (docs/vendor/bloodbowlbase/.../the_game_of_blood_bowl/index.html, RESOLVE PASS ACTION, clean

with no errata) states "If the ball lands in an unoccupied square, then it will Bounce from that square" — and the engine already implements that bounce for ACCURATE passes to empty squares (proc_ball.c:231, tested by ball_pass_accurate_to_empty_square_bounces at test_rules_ball.c:346), making the inaccurate-branch rest at line 59 an internal inconsistency, exactly as the file header (proc_ball.c:17-21) documents the intended behavior. title: Side Step with no free adjacent square hands the attacker's push-square choice to the defending coach
claim: push_advance assigns the phase-0 decision to the defender's team whenever the defender has Side Step (and no Grab/Juggernaut), without checking that Side Step is actually usable (a free adjacent square exists). push_legal's Side Step branch falls through to the normal three push candidates when no square is free (line 548 comment), so the DEFENDING coach ends up choosing among the attacker's three push squares — including chain vs crowd choices — a decision the rules give to the attacker. In a surrounded-cage scenario the defender picks the square most favorable to themselves (wrong outcomes in realistic play), and the decision_team attribution diverges from FUMBBL replays, breaking BC corpus extraction and the self-play perm/tags for exactly the crowded positions where it matters most.
evidence: Trace: push_advance phase 0 (proc_block.c:402-408): side_step = (def_flags & BB_PUSHF_SIDE_STEP) && !jugg && !grab, then bb_need_decision(m, side_step ? BB_TEAM_OF(f->b) : m->active_team) — no availability check. push_legal (lines 530-549): the Side Step branch enumerates unoccupied adjacent squares; 'if (n > 0) return n; // No free square: fall through to the normal three candidates' (lines 547-548) — the standard candidates (possibly chain arg=2 / crowd arg=1 entries, lines 561-572) are then surfaced to whichever team push_advance selected, which is still the defender. bb_apply (bb_match.c:281-306) validates only set membership, not decision ownership semantics, so the defender's policy answers the prompt. Reachable whenever a Side Step player is fully surrounded (all 8 neighbours occupied or off-pitch) — the standard crowd-surf-the-Sidestepper position.
fix: In push_advance phase 0, compute Side Step availability before assigning the decision owner: side_step = side_step && (exists adjacent on-pitch square with !m->grid[x][y]). If none, fall back to bb_need_decision(m, m->active_team). Mirror the same availability predicate in push_legal so legal/advance can never disagree.

BB_TEAM_OF(f->b) : m->active_team) — bb_hook_push_flags (bb_hooks.c:82-91) only ORs static skill-table flags, so there is NO free-adjacent-square availability check before ownership is assigned. (2) push_legal (proc_block.c:535-548) gates on the identical skill predicate, enumerates unoccupied adjacent on-pitch squares, and only returns them if n>0; with the defender fully surrounded it falls through to the standard three candidates (lines 550-573), which are then all chain (arg=2) or crowd (arg=1) entries — the attacker's choice set, surfaced under decision_team = defender. (3) bb_apply (bb_match.c:281-306) validates set membership only, and the RL binding routes the decision to m->decision_team (puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:419 and the mask gate at :298-299), so the DEFENDING policy genuinely picks the attacker's chain-vs-crowd push square. (4) The project's authoritative BB2025 mirror (docs/vendor/bloodbowlbase/.../bb2025/core_rules/skills_and_traits/index.html) states: "If there are no adjacent unoccupied squares, then this Skill cannot be used" — i.e. the default rule ("the opposing Coach choosing where this player is Pushed Back to") applies, giving the choice to the ATTACKER. title: Hypnotic Gaze / Animal Savagery Distracted is never cleared for players without an activation-gate negatrait — persists to end of drive
claim: BB_PF_DISTRACTED set by Hypnotic Gaze (proc_move.c:899) or an Animal Savagery lash-out (proc_turn.c:357) is permanent for the rest of the drive for any player lacking a Bone Head/Really Stupid/Unchannelled Fury/Take Root gate, instead of clearing at the player's next activation as the engine's own flag documentation specifies (bb_types.h:70 'until end of next activation'). The victim loses tackle zones, marking, catching, hand-off receiving, assists, AND their Block/Wrestle/Dodge block-face effects for up to 16 team turns.
evidence: Exhaustive grep of all writes: BB_PF_DISTRACTED is SET at proc_move.c:899 (gaze 3+), proc_turn.c:190 (gate failure), proc_turn.c:357 (Animal Savagery); it is CLEARED only at proc_turn.c:199 — which sits INSIDE the `if (bb_hook_activation_gate(m, slot, &target, &gk) >= 0 ...)` block at proc_turn.c:180, i.e. only players whose skillset contains a registered activate_gate (skills_core.c:88-95,129: BONE_HEAD, REALLY_STUPID, UNCHANNELLED_FURY, TAKE_ROOT) ever reach it — and at proc_match.c:697 (`p->flags = 0` in end_drive_advance). activation_advance's wrap-up (proc_turn.c:208-211) and turn_start (proc_turn.c:21-22) clear other flags but not DISTRACTED. Downstream: bb_exerts_tz (bb_match.c:138) and bb_can_catch (bb_match.c:160) return false while Distracted, and proc_block.c:288-306 cancels Block/Wrestle for Distracted players. Reachable every Vampire activation: all three Vampire positionals carry BB_SK_HYPNOTIC_GAZE (gen_teams.c:299-301), GAZE is offered every activation (proc_turn.c:271-273) and succeeds on 3+ (proc_move.c:898); the Rat Ogre (gen_teams.c:65,259) self-inflicts the same permanent Distracted via Animal Savagery since it has no activation gate.
fix: Clear BB_PF_DISTRACTED unconditionally when a player activates — e.g. at the top of activation_advance phase 0 (next to the BB_PF_EYE_GOUGED clear at proc_turn.c:176), or in the activation wrap-up at proc_turn.c:208-211 — and let gate failure re-set it (proc_turn.c:190 already runs after that point). Then delete the now-redundant gate-success clear at proc_turn.c:197-200.

title: Stunty dodger vs Titchy marker: two independent cancellations of the same -1 stack to a net +1 dodge bonus
claim: When a Stunty player dodges into a square marked by a Titchy opponent, both skills cancel the SAME base -1 marking modifier and the sums stack: base -1 (proc_move.c:279/750) + Stunty +1 (skills_core.c:177, counts ALL markers including Titchy ones) + Titchy aura +1 (skills_devious_traits.c:48) = net +1 per Titchy marker. The correct combined result of two 'ignore/withhold the -1' effects is 0, so the dodger rolls one pip better than any reading of the rules allows.
evidence: Code path: proc_move.c:750 computes mod = -bb_tackle_zones(dest) + bb_hook_mods(ctx{DODGE,to=dest}). bb_hook_mods (bb_hooks.c:22-53) sums the dodger's own STUNTY mod — skills_core.c:175-178 returns bb_tackle_zones(m, team, c->to_x, c->to_y), which counts every opposing exerts-tz marker of the destination including Titchy ones (bb_tackle_zones, bb_match.c:142-154, has no skill filter) — plus the opposing TITCHY aura, skills_devious_traits.c:39-49, which returns +1 for each Titchy opponent with cheb==1 to the destination. Both hooks fire on the same test; nothing dedupes them. Reachable on stock rosters: Snotling Lineman (gen_teams.c:265,286), Gnoblar Lineman (:210), Beer Boar (:191) and Fungus Flinga (:268) all carry BOTH BB_SK_STUNTY and BB_SK_TITCHY, so any Stunty-roster mirror (Snotling v Snotling) or Goblin/Halfling v Snotling match hits it; ~140K-match fuzzing with procgen rosters hits it constantly.
fix: Make the Titchy aura abstain when the acting player ignores marking modifiers anyway: in skills_devious_traits.c:39-49 add `if (bb_has_skill(&m->players[c->player].skills, BB_SK_STUNTY)) return 0;` (or equivalently make Stunty's cancellation count only non-Titchy markers, but the aura-side guard is the one-line fix).
