title: Diving Tackle auto-interrupt fires on dodges that do NOT leave the diver's tackle zone (eligibility check missing, unlike Shadowing)
claim: The Diving Tackle auto-policy (D19) selects any opposing DT player marking the dodger's CURRENT square (proc_move.c:217-229) without checking that the dodge destination actually leaves that player's tackle zone. A dodge from one square of a DT player's TZ to another square of the SAME player's TZ wrongly gets flipped from pass to fail (m->ret &= ~1 at :230), knocking the mover down with a turnover and placing the diver prone in the vacated square — for a skill use that is not legal in that situation per the rule text the repo itself quotes.
evidence: proc_move.c:216-229: eligibility is only `bb_slot_at` adjacent to p->x/p->y + opposing team + bb_exerts_tz + has DIVING_TACKLE + not ball carrier; the step destination (still available in f->x/f->y, set at proc_move.c:727-728) is never consulted. The repo's own rule quote at skills_agility.c:22-24 reads 'When an opposition player attempts to LEAVE THIS PLAYER'S TACKLE ZONE as a result of a Dodge...'. The directly parallel Shadowing implementation, triggered by the same leave-TZ condition, performs exactly this check: proc_move.c:80 `if (bb_adjacent(nx0, ny0, x, y)) continue; // Still adjacent to the mover's new square? Then no need.` Reachability: dodging around a blocker within one marker's TZ is routine, and DIVING_TACKLE is in the agility random-skill table (gen_skills.c:118) so procgen training squads carry it regularly.
fix: In the diver scan at proc_move.c:217-229, require that the destination leaves the candidate's TZ: `if (bb_adjacent(m->players[dts].x, m->players[dts].y, f->x, f->y)) continue;` (mirroring the Shadowing check at proc_move.c:80).
votes: UPHELD: Claim survives. Code-path trace verified exactly: at proc_move.c:727-732 the dodge test is triggered solely by the vacated square being marked (bb_tackle_zones at p->x,p->y), so a dodge that stays inside the same DT player's TZ still rolls; at test resolution (proc_move.c:204-235) the mover has not yet moved, and the DT scan at :217-229 checks only adjacency to p->x/p->y (vacated square) + opposing/TZ/skill/not-carrier — f->x/f->y (the destination, live in the frame from :727-728) is never consulted. The flip at :230 (m->ret &= ~1) then routes into the failure branch (:251-271): mover knocked down at the destination with a turnover, diver placed prone in the vacated square. Die/target extraction matches proc_test.c:46/58/65 packing. Concrete reachable repro: AG3+ mover at (10,10) marked only by DT diver at (10,11) steps to (11,11) — dodge required, target 4+, roll 4 passes, die!=6 and 4-2<4, so DT wrongly fires despite the mover never leaving the diver's TZ. Rules adjudication favors the claim: the repo's own BB2025 mirror (docs/vendor/bloodbowlbase/.../skills_and_traits/index.html:6932) says DT triggers when a player "attempts to leave this player's Tackle Zone", in deliberate contrast to Shadowing's per-square wording (:6979 "Dodge out of a square within this player's Tackle Zone"); and decisively, FFB — the project's stated final-word replay-differential oracle — implements the exact missing check BY DEFAULT (vendor/ffb UtilPlayer.java:655-668 filters divers still adjacent to the destination; GameOptionFactory.java:87-90 defaults DIVING_TACKLE_LEAVING_TZ_ONLY to true: "Diving Tackle only when the dodger leaves the tackler's TZ"), guaranteeing Layer-7 replay divergences. Not shielded by D19, which documents the auto-USE policy (only when -2 flips a pass, never the carrier), not the eligibility footprint, and explicitly defers fidelity to the FUMBBL differential. Caveats that weaken but do not refute: Jervis BB2025 (vendor/jervis-ffb DodgeRoll.kt:411-423) applies no destination filter (matches the engine), and the rulebook's Shadowing ¶3 reuses "leave the Tackle Zone" loosely, so genuine ambiguity exists (hence FFB's game option) — but FUMBBL's default behavior is the leaving-TZ-only reading, which is this repo's ground truth. Minor evidence flaw: the claim mislabels the Shadowing check at proc_move.c:80 as the "same leave-TZ condition" (it is an auto-policy "no need" heuristic; Shadowing's trigger is per-square) — cosmetic, does not affect the finding. Suggested fix (skip divers where bb_adjacent(diver, f->x, f->y)) mirrors FFB's default filter exactly. MEDIUM severity is appropriate: wrongly converts passing dodges into knockdown+turnover in a routine geometry (dodging around a DT marker), corrupting both rules fidelity and RL training signal.

title: Catch reroll and Nerves of Steel incorrectly apply to interception attempts (BB2025 Intercept != Catch)
claim: Interception tests are dispatched with kind BB_TEST_CATCH, so BB_SKILL_REROLL(CATCH, 1u << BB_TEST_CATCH) (skills_core.c:22) grants the interceptor a free skill reroll and NERVES_OF_STEEL (skills_core.c:61-65) cancels the interceptor's marking penalty. BB2025 treats Intercept as a distinct action from Catch: the mirror's Catch text is 're-roll any failed Agility Test when attempting to Catch the ball', NoS is 'ignore any modifiers for being Marked when making an Agility Test to Catch the ball, or when making a Passing Ability Test to Pass the ball' (no Intercept clause), and Disturbing Presence's text 'attempts to Intercept or Catch the ball' proves the two are separate terms. A Catch-skill interceptor therefore roughly doubles its interception odds, and interceptions auto-fire whenever there is a single candidate (D10), so this skews realistic self-play and will diverge in the FUMBBL/FFB differential.
evidence: Trace: pass_start_interception (engine/src/proc_ball.c:358-365) builds bb_ctx{BB_TEST_CATCH, interceptor, f->a(thrower), ...} and pushes BB_PROC_TEST with kind BB_TEST_CATCH. On a failed roll, test_advance (engine/src/proc_test.c:51) and test_legal (proc_test.c:98) call bb_skill_reroll_for(m, slot, f->b) -> bb_hook_reroll(m, slot, kind) (engine/src/bb_hooks.c:55-66), which sees only the kind, not the ctx, so it cannot use the c->other != BB_NO_PLAYER interception discriminator that STUNTY (skills_core.c:179) and VERY_LONG_LEGS (skills_mutation_passing.c:80) use, and returns BB_SK_CATCH. Separately, bb_hook_mods on that ctx invokes the NoS mod (skills_core.c:61-65) which returns +bb_tackle_zones at the interceptor's square for any BB_TEST_CATCH, cancelling the '-1 per marker' the base computes at proc_ball.c:360. Rule texts verified in docs/vendor/bloodbowlbase/bloodbowlbase.ru/bb2025/core_rules/skills_and_traits/index.html (raw HTML offsets ~114474 CATCH, ~135342 NERVES OF STEEL, ~128419 DISTURBING PRESENCE; the file contains zero <del> errata tags). Note the engine already implements the BB2025-correct behaviour for EXTRA_ARMS ('Catch, Pick Up or Intercept') and STUNTY/VLL, so this is an inconsistency, not a wholesale design choice; no DECISIONS.md entry covers it.
fix: Add a guard in the NoS hook: if (c->kind == BB_TEST_CATCH && c->other != BB_NO_PLAYER) return 0;. For the reroll: either introduce a dedicated BB_TEST_INTERCEPT kind used by pass_start_interception (and add it to STUNTY/VLL/EXTRA_ARMS/DP filters), or pass the bb_ctx (not just kind) to bb_hook_reroll so Catch can decline when other != BB_NO_PLAYER. Add a unit test asserting a Catch-skill interceptor gets no reroll offer and a marked NoS interceptor keeps its -1.

(2) NoS path: proc_ball.c:360 applies the rulebook's -1 per marker, then bb_hook_mods (proc_ball.c:361 -> bb_hooks.c:22-35) invokes NERVES_OF_STEEL (skills_core.c:61-65), which gates only on kind==PASS||CATCH and returns +bb_tackle_zones at the interceptor's square — exact cancellation, no other!=BB_NO_PLAYER guard. (3) Rules: the project's canonical mirror (docs/vendor/bloodbowlbase/.../bb2025/core_rules/skills_and_traits/index.html, 0 <del> tags, FAQ silent) gives NoS as 'Catch the ball, or ... title: Disturbing Presence can never fire on Throw Team-mate: proc_ttm computes its PA test without consulting bb_hook_mods
claim: BB2025 Disturbing Presence applies '-1 ... for each player with this Skill within 3 squares' to 'Any opposition player that performs a Pass Action, Throw Team-mate Action or a Throw Bomb Special Action, or attempts to Intercept or Catch the ball'. The DP aura registered at skills_core.c:71-83 only fires through bb_hook_mods, but proc_ttm.c computes the TTM Passing Ability Test entirely inline (range mod, marking, Strong Arm, then the die) without ever building a bb_ctx or calling bb_hook_mods, so DP's -1 is silently never applied to TTM throws (and no other registered mod/aura, nor any reroll window, can affect that test either). TTM is core play for Ogre/Snotling/Goblin/Black Orc/Troll teams, which are in the procgen rotation and the golden-trace matchups; DP carriers (e.g. Chaos Dwarf Flamesmith, plus procgen mutation advancement) make the missing -1 reachable in realistic matches.
evidence: Trace: ttm_advance (engine/src/proc_ttm.c:59-75) computes mod = range(0/-1) - bb_tackle_zones(...) + Strong Arm inline and rolls bb_d6 directly; grep confirms proc_ttm.c contains no bb_ctx, no bb_hook_mods, and no BB_PROC_TEST push. The DP hook (engine/src/skills_core.c:71-83) filters on c->kind == BB_TEST_PASS || BB_TEST_CATCH and is dispatched only from bb_hook_mods (engine/src/bb_hooks.c:37-51), so there is no code path by which DP can modify a TTM throw. Rule text verified in the BB2025 mirror (skills_and_traits/index.html, DISTURBING PRESENCE paragraph: 'performs a Pass Action, Throw Team-mate Action or a Throw Bomb Special Action...'). The in-source DP comment quotes an older text ('make a Passing Ability Test, or attempt to Catch or Intercept') that omits TTM, which is why the registration's own filter also lacks it. Not covered by any DECISIONS.md auto-policy (D21 covers TTM-adjacent items but not this).
fix: Route the TTM PA test through the hook dispatcher: build a bb_ctx with a dedicated kind (e.g. BB_TEST_TTM) at proc_ttm.c:59 and add that kind to the DP filter in skills_core.c:72 (keeping Accurate/Cannoneer/Pass-reroll excluded since TTM is not a Pass Action). This also creates the natural seam for the missing team-reroll window on TTM tests. Add a unit test: thrower with an opposing DP player 3 squares away must need one pip more.

(2) DP registers only an .aura hook (BB_SKILL_AURA macro, bb_hooks.h:83-88) whose sole consumer is bb_hook_mods (bb_hooks.c:37-51); its filter at skills_core.c:72 accepts only BB_TEST_PASS/BB_TEST_CATCH. (3) ttm_advance (proc_ttm.c:59-75) computes the TTM PA test fully inline (range mod, bb_tackle_zones marking, Strong Arm, direct bb_d6) — grep confirms proc_ttm.c contains no bb_ctx, no bb_hook_mods, no BB_PROC_TEST (only an unused bb_hooks.h include at line 26), and the sole push site proc_move.c:818 calls no hooks first. title: Big Hand fails to cancel the Pouring Rain pickup modifier (BB2025: ignores ALL negative pickup modifiers)
claim: The BB2025 Big Hand text is 'This player ignores all negative modifiers when attempting to pick up the ball', but the hook at skills_core.c:41-44 returns only +bb_tackle_zones at the ball square (cancelling marking), while both pickup call sites apply the Pouring Rain -1 AFTER bb_hook_mods returns, so a Big Hand player still suffers -1 picking up in rain. The in-source comment paraphrases the rule as 'ignore Marking modifiers when picking up the ball', which does not match the mirror text, so the rulebook-test campaign would not have caught it. Reachable in training: no base roster carries Big Hand, but procgen advancement rolls it from the Mutation column (bb_random_skill_table[BB_CAT_MUTATION][0] = BB_SK_BIG_HAND, gen_skills.c:121) for any position with Mutation primary access (e.g. Beastman primary_mask 17 = GENERAL|MUTATION, gen_teams.c Chaos Chosen), and rain occurs via the weather roll plus the implemented Changing Weather kickoff event.
evidence:
Trace: pickup sites build bb_ctx{BB_TEST_PICKUP,...} then compute mod = -bb_tackle_zones(...) + bb_hook_mods(...), and only afterwards do 'if (m->weather == BB_WEATHER_RAIN) mod -= 1;' (engine/src/proc_move.c:116-122 and 194-197), so no hook can see or cancel the rain term. The hook (skills_core.c:41-44) returns bb_tackle_zones(m, team, c->to_x, c->to_y) only. Rule text verified in the raw BB2025 mirror HTML (docs/vendor/bloodbowlbase/.../skills_and_traits/index.html offset ~128419: '

This player ignores all negative modifiers when attempting to pick up the ball.

', no errata). Marking and rain are the only negative pickup modifiers in the engine, so the divergence is exactly the rain case.
fix: Make the rain term hook-visible: apply the weather -1 into mod BEFORE calling bb_hook_mods and let BIG_HAND return (marking_tz + (weather==RAIN ? 1 : 0)) for BB_TEST_PICKUP; or add a bb_skills.h query (precedent: bb_is_stunty) like bb_pickup_ignores_negmods() consulted at the two pickup sites to clamp mod to >= 0 before the weather subtraction. Add a unit test: Big Hand pickup in Pouring Rain while double-marked must be a plain AG target with no negative modifiers.

title: Obs bytes b[6]/b[7] encode team ids and flags as ego-remapped player slots — the 'frame a/b are player slots in every proc that surfaces decisions' premise is false
claim: bbe_encode_obs (bloodbowl.h:203-204) treats top-frame a/b as player slots whenever the value is < BB_NUM_PLAYERS (32) and ego-remaps them into obs bytes b[6]/b[7]. But the four highest-frequency decision-surfacing procs store TEAM ids (0/1) in frame a, not slots: PREGAME coin toss (proc_match.c:121-123, f->a = toss-winner team), SETUP (proc_match.c:34, a = kicking_team; surfaces every placement decision via bb_need_decision at proc_match.c:272), KICKOFF (proc_match.c:40 + comment proc_match.c:337 'a = kicking team'; surfaces kick-target/touchback/High Kick), and TEAM_TURN (proc_turn.c:6 'a = team', pushed at proc_match.c:61; surfaces every ACTIVATE/END_TURN decision via bb_need_decision at proc_turn.c:100). CASUALTY frames similarly carry a non-slot flag in b (proc_block.c:868 pushes b=1). Since 0/1 < 32, the binding remaps them as slots: during the home team's own turn b[6] = 1+bbe_ego_slot(0,0) = 1 ('my player 0'), while during the away team's own turn b[6] = 1+bbe_ego_slot(1,1) = 18 ('opponent row 17'). The same semantic state yields different feature values per side — breaking the egocentric invariant the whole obs design is built on — and the bogus values alias genuine slot encodings used by BLOCK/TEST/PUSH frames on the same byte channel. This affects the modal decision type (ACTIVATE) plus all setup/kickoff/pregame decisions, i.e. the majority of steps in every match.
evidence: Code path: bb_push stores a verbatim (engine/src/bb_match.c:25 'f->a = (uint8_t)a'). proc_match.c:61 'bb_push(m, BB_PROC_TEAM_TURN, next, 0, 0, 0)' with next = team id; proc_turn.c:76-101 team_turn_advance calls bb_need_decision(m, team) with the TEAM_TURN frame on top, so at every ACTIVATE decision m->stack[stack_top-1].a is the team id. bloodbowl.h:194 takes that top frame; line 203 'b[6] = top->a < BB_NUM_PLAYERS ? (unsigned char)(1 + bbe_ego_slot(me, top->a)) : 0' — 0 and 1 both pass the <32 check and get XOR-16 ego remapping meant for slots (bloodbowl.h:142-144). The justifying comment at bloodbowl.h:199-200 ('Frame a/b are player slots in every proc that surfaces decisions') is contradicted by proc_match.c:34/40/121 and proc_turn.c:6.

title: CHOOSE_OPTION args that are player slots (interception pick, High Kick placement) bypass ego remap — away agent must select its own players via opponent-range arg indices
claim: bbe_arg_is_slot (bloodbowl.h:257-267) limits egocentric arg remapping to SETUP_PLACE/SETUP_REMOVE/TOUCHBACK/ACTIVATE. But two engine decision points emit BB_A_CHOOSE_OPTION actions whose arg IS a raw player slot: the multi-candidate interception window (engine/src/proc_ball.c:349 'out[n++] = (bb_action){BB_A_CHOOSE_OPTION, cands[i], 0, 0}', cands = defending-team slots from ruler_candidates, decision given to the defending coach at proc_ball.c:297) and the High Kick player placement (engine/src/proc_match.c:595, receiving-team slots, decided by the receiving coach). When the deciding coach is AWAY, its candidate players are slots 16..31, which pass through bbe_action_arg unremapped (bloodbowl.h:283-290: not in bbe_arg_is_slot, 16..31 < 32, returned verbatim) — so the away agent must pick its own interceptor via arg-head indices 16-31, the range that means 'opponent players' in its obs rows and in every other slot-typed action (ACTIVATE/TOUCHBACK/SETUP). The home agent gets indices 0-15 for the identical decision. Encode (bbe_fill_mask:307) and decode (bbe_decode:320) use the same mapping, so no illegal action results — but the arg head's semantics flip by side and contradict the obs egocentric layout, degrading the weight-shared self-play policy on every multi-candidate interception and High Kick across a 2B-step run. Note the engine also violates its own contract here: bb_actions.h:57-58 documents CHOOSE_OPTION arg as 'index into a procedure-defined option list', not a player slot.
evidence: Trace for an away-defended pass: proc_ball.c:294-298 — nc>1 → bb_need_decision(m, 1 - BB_TEAM_OF(slot)) with thrower home → decision_team = BB_AWAY; pass_legal (proc_ball.c:342-353) emits CHOOSE_OPTION with cands[i] ∈ 16..31 (ruler_candidates at proc_ball.c:202 keeps only slots with BB_TEAM_OF(s) != BB_TEAM_OF(thrower)). Binding: bbe_action_arg(1, a) at bloodbowl.h:283-290 — bbe_arg_is_slot(BB_A_CHOOSE_OPTION) is false (switch at 257-267), arg 16..31 < 32 → returned raw, so mask bits 16..31 of the arg head are set for the away agent's own players, whereas bbe_encode_obs rows 0..15 hold those same players (bloodbowl.h:152-156 with me=1).
fix: Remap CHOOSE_OPTION slot payloads in the binding by consulting the top frame: when match.stack top is BB_PROC_PASS (interception window) or BB_PROC_KICKOFF phase 4 (High Kick), treat arg < BB_NUM_PLAYERS as a slot in bbe_action_arg/bbe_decode (pass the env or top-proc into bbe_action_arg). Cleaner: change the engine to emit the candidate INDEX (0..nc-1) per bb_actions.h's documented CHOOSE_OPTION contract and resolve the slot in pass_apply/kickoff_apply — then no binding change is needed. Add a both-sides unit test that the arg-head index of an interception candidate equals its obs row.

title: Setup-phase decisions materialize and walk the ~3120-action placement cross-product up to 4x per placement, dominating random-policy env time
claim: DECISIONS.md D2 assumes the RL heads 'never materialize' the setup placement list, but the binding does materialize it for every setup decision — and then walks it repeatedly. Each SETUP_PLACE step costs ~12K+ list-entry visits (~25-45us) versus the 4.85us average step, and under D1's 64-action budget a random/early policy takes ~130 setup decisions per drive, making setup plausibly the single largest time sink in the standalone benchmark (rough arithmetic: ~390 setup steps x ~30us ≈ 12ms vs ~20ms for the whole 4096-decision match).
evidence: Trace per setup decision: (1) bbe_refresh_legal (bloodbowl.h:132-136) calls setup_legal (proc_match.c:278-312) which loops 16 players x ~190 own-half squares emitting ~3120 bb_action entries; (2) bbe_fill_mask (bloodbowl.h:304-309) iterates all ~3120 entries calling bbe_action_arg + bbe_action_sq per entry to set only ~210 distinct mask bits (16 args + ~190 squares + a few types); (3) bbe_decode pass 1 (bloodbowl.h:317-323) scans up to ~3120 entries, with passes 2-3 (326-332) re-scanning on a miss; (4) bb_apply (bb_match.c:288) re-runs the ~3120-entry setup_legal enumeration plus an average ~1560-entry validation scan. That is 4+ traversals of a ~3120-entry list per single placement action. Frequency: D1 budget = 64 placement actions per team per setup (SETUP_ACTION_BUDGET, proc_match.c:266), two teams per drive, ~3+ drives per random match; even a trained policy pays ~24 setup steps (11 placements + DONE per side) per drive at this per-step cost.
fix: Fast-path the setup proc in the binding: when stack top is BB_PROC_SETUP, fill the arg head (placeable/placed players) and square head (free own-half squares) directly from the same conditions setup_legal uses, without materializing the cross-product, and decode by directly validating the sampled (slot, x, y) against those conditions (constructing {BB_A_SETUP_PLACE, slot, x, y}). Combined with the bb_apply_trusted fix this removes all four O(n) traversals. A less invasive partial fix: bb_apply_trusted alone removes traversal (4); capping SETUP_ACTION_BUDGET lower shrinks worst-case frequency (D1 already invites revisiting the budget size).

(2) The 4+ traversals are real: bbe_fill_mask walks all entries calling bbe_action_arg+bbe_action_sq (bloodbowl.h:307-312, deciding agent only — claim doesn't overcount); bbe_decode pass 1 scans up to all entries (320-325) with passes 2-3 rescanning on miss (329-337); bb_apply re-runs the full setup_legal enumeration plus a linear bb_action_eq validation scan (bb_match.c:287-295). UPHELD: The claim survives the reachability attack — the expensive path is hit constantly in real runs at the claimed frequency, and every cited traversal exists in the code. Verified traces: (1) setup decisions recur every drive — match_advance (engine/src/proc_match.c:21-100) pushes BB_PROC_SETUP at the start of each half AND after every TD (case 4 resets f->phase=1), so >=2 setups per match minimum; (2) the 64-action budget genuinely burns under random masked sampling — setup_constraints_ok (proc_match.c:172-193) requires exactly min(11,avail) on pitch, >=3 on the 7 LoS-column center squares (P~0.8% under random placement), and <=2 per wide zone, so SETUP_DONE is almost never legal (proc_match.c:289) before f->data >= SETUP_ACTION_BUDGET (proc_match.c:266,285) forces it, yielding ~65 decisions/team/setup = ~130/drive exactly as claimed (this is D1's documented livelock behavior); (3) all four O(n) traversals per placement are real: bbe_refresh_legal (puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:135-139) fully materializes setup_legal's cross-product (proc_match.c:277-312, 195 own-half squares per player), bbe_fill_mask (bloodbowl.h:307-312) walks every entry to set only ~200 distinct bits, bbe_decode pass 1 (bloodbowl.h:320-325) scans ~n/2 (full n + pass-2 rescan when the sq head samples 390 with PLACE or a real square with REMOVE — making the claim conservative), and bb_apply (engine/src/bb_match.c:282-308) verifiably re-runs bb_legal_actions into a fresh stack buffer plus a linear bb_action_eq scan; (4) the arithmetic is internally consistent with the repo's own measurement — STATUS.md:3 records 206K env-steps/sec/core (= the 4.85us baseline), and even at the 2-drive minimum, 260 setup steps x ~25-40us is ~35-60% of a ~20ms 4096-decision episode, with no other proc having legal lists beyond tens of entries. The path is exercised by the exact standalone benchmark that produced the 206K figure (bloodbowl.c:66-69 masked sampling), torch practice runs, early CUDA training (random-init policy), and the bb_apply side by all ~140K fuzz matches. Minor corrections that do not change the verdict: procgen squads are 11-14 players, not 16 (engine/src/bb_procgen.c:40 'squad = 11 + pg_pick(rng, 4)'; remaining slots BB_LOC_ABSENT), so real lists are ~2,100-2,700 entries rather than ~3,120 (a 15-30% overcount, same order of magnitude); cited line numbers are off by ~3; and D2 is slightly misread (its 'heads never materialize' statement is literally true of the factored policy heads) — but D2 only addresses the 16KB memory cost, not the 4-traversals-per-step throughput interaction with D1's budget, so this is new information, not a re-flag of a documented decision.

REFUTED: The 4-traversal trace is mechanically accurate (verified: bbe_refresh_legal at bloodbowl.h:135-139 → bb_legal_actions at engine/src/bb_match.c:273-279 → setup_legal at engine/src/proc_match.c:278-312 emitting ~3000 entries; bbe_fill_mask loop at bloodbowl.h:307-313; bbe_decode passes at :320-337; bb_apply re-enumeration/validation at bb_match.c:287-299), but the finding is refuted under the design-intent lens on four grounds. (1) The D2 framing is a misreading: D2 (DECISIONS.md:10) says the RL action HEADS never materialize the list — meaning the policy's output space is the factored {30,33,391} of D22 (DECISIONS.md:37, docs/policy-research.md), not a 4096-way head — and that remains true. The same D2 sentence explicitly blesses per-call enumeration cost ('16KB stack per enumeration is fine'); env-side materialization into env->legal (bloodbowl.h:130) is the intended mask-filling machinery (masked factored heads require enumerating the legal set per decision — policy-research.md §2), acknowledged again at bloodbowl.c:34 ('~20KB of legal-action buffer'). No documented assumption is violated. (2) No perf budget is violated — the opposite: the 4.85us/step figure the claim cites IS the measured standalone random-policy benchmark (STATUS.md:3: 206K env-steps/sec/core, 2.1M/sec aggregate vs a 500K target, beaten ~4x), and that measurement already INCLUDES every setup traversal described (bloodbowl.c plays full random matches through the exact c_step path). Phase 5's throughput milestone was closed with this cost priced in; 'setup dominates a benchmark that passes its documented target 4x' is an optimization note, not a MEDIUM defect. (3) Both proposed fixes break documented invariants: the binding-side fast-path re-derives setup legality conditions, creating a second source of truth that can drift from the engine (e.g., the budget-exhausted DONE-only state at proc_match.c:284-287, 'pitch full' rule at :299, self-square exclusion at :303) — exactly the binding-vs-engine divergence class the prior deep review fixed as HIGH ('unsound spatial discriminator'); any drift makes bb_apply return BB_STATUS_ERROR and c_step silently resets the episode mid-match (bloodbowl.h:435-437), corrupting training to save microseconds. And bb_apply_trusted removes the explicitly named 'mask-soundness invariant' validation (bb_match.c:286) that CLAUDE.md:16 requires because masks exist ONLY in the CUDA backend (the D24 maskless torch path depends on the env tolerating illegal actions), and that all ~140K fuzzed matches exercised. (4) The 'dominating time sink' quantification (~25-45us/setup-step, ~390 setup steps, ~3 drives) is unmeasured back-of-envelope arithmetic, and its frequency premise is a transient random-policy artifact — the claim itself concedes trained policies pay only ~24 setup steps/drive. D23 (DECISIONS.md:38) records the project's documented stance on precisely this class: a known random-policy-only decode artifact was consciously documented-not-fixed because it 'vanishes as the policy sharpens.' A correct trace of an explicitly accepted cost, hung on a misread decision, with fixes that break a documented safety invariant, against a perf target met 4x over, does not stand as a finding.

title: fx_player's memset makes every fixture placement wipe grid[0][0], corrupting board state when (0,0) is occupied
claim: fx_player zeroes the player struct before calling bb_place, which sets location = 0 = BB_LOC_ON_PITCH and x = y = 0. bb_place's stale-square cleanup then unconditionally clears m->grid[0][0] on EVERY fx_player call. If an earlier fixture call placed a player at (0,0), that player's grid occupancy entry is silently erased: the grid says (0,0) is empty while the player struct says on-pitch at (0,0). This is live today: test movement_cannot_step_off_pitch (test_rules_movement.c:137) places the mover at (0,0) and then calls fx_lineman(&m, 1, 9, 24, 14) at line 138 — after fixture setup the mover under test has no grid entry. The test's current assertions (count of step destinations) happen not to consult the mover's own square so it passes green, but the position being tested is corrupt: any opponent could legally step onto the 'empty' (0,0), tackle-zone/occupancy logic that scans the grid sees no player there, and any future assertion added to this or any (0,0)-using fixture silently tests the wrong state.
evidence: Trace: bb_fixtures.h:51 `memset(p, 0, sizeof(*p))` -> p->location = 0; bb_types.h:50 `BB_LOC_ON_PITCH = 0`; bb_fixtures.h:57 calls bb_place; bb_match.c:52-54 `if (p->location == BB_LOC_ON_PITCH) { m->grid[p->x][p->y] = 0; }` with p->x = p->y = 0 from the memset, so grid[0][0] is cleared before the new placement at bb_match.c:58. Trigger: test_rules_movement.c:137 `int mover = fx_lineman(&m, 0, 0, 0, 0);` (grid[0][0] = mover+1) followed by line 138 `fx_lineman(&m, 1, 9, 24, 14);` whose bb_place first executes grid[0][0] = 0, erasing the mover's entry. fx_match_midturn sets all players to BB_LOC_ABSENT (bb_fixtures.h:25-27) precisely to avoid this, but fx_player's memset undoes it for the slot being placed.
fix: In fx_player, after the memset and before bb_place, add `p->location = BB_LOC_ABSENT;` so bb_place's on-pitch cleanup branch is skipped. Then add a fixture-level assertion test that places a player at (0,0), places a second player, and checks m.grid[0][0] still maps to the first player.
votes: UPHELD: Claim survives full code-path trace; every evidence link verified. (1) bb_fixtures.h:50 (claim cites 51 — trivial off-by-one; memset is line 50) zeroes the whole bb_player, and bb_types.h:50 confirms BB_LOC_ON_PITCH = 0, so the slot being placed enters bb_place with location=ON_PITCH, x=y=0; nothing in fx_player lines 51-56 reassigns location. (2) bb_place (engine/src/bb_match.c:52-54) then executes the stale-square cleanup `m->grid[p->x][p->y] = 0` with x=y=0, i.e. EVERY fx_player call clears grid[0][0] before placing at bb_match.c:58. (3) The live trigger is real: test_rules_movement.c:137 places the mover (slot 0) at (0,0) giving grid[0][0]=1; line 138 fx_lineman(&m,1,9,24,14) (slot 25) memsets slot 25 and its bb_place wipes grid[0][0], leaving players[0] on-pitch at (0,0) with no grid entry. (4) Nothing heals it: grid is written only by bb_place (bb_match.c:53,58) and bb_remove_from_pitch (bb_match.c:64); proc_turn.c has zero grid references, so the turn_start that runs inside begin_move (test_rules_movement.c:32-38) does not rebuild occupancy — the desync persists through the assertions. (5) The claim's pass-despite-corruption explanation is correct: count_steps only counts BB_A_STEP destinations, step enumeration (proc_move.c:428) skips occupied squares via grid, and the mover's own square is never an adjacent destination (bb_match.c:47 requires (dx|dy)!=0), so the (1,0)/(0,1)/(1,1) count of 3 is unaffected — test stays green on a corrupt position. (6) Consequences are accurately characterized: step legality is grid-only (proc_move.c:428) so an adjacent opponent would be offered the occupied (0,0), and bb_slot_at (bb_match.h:150) is grid-backed so TZ/occupancy logic misses the mover. (7) Not by design: DECISIONS.md D1-D24 say nothing about fixtures; fx_match_midturn setting all players BB_LOC_ABSENT (bb_fixtures.h:25-27) demonstrates the intended invariant that fx_player's memset defeats. Proposed fix (set p->location = BB_LOC_ABSENT after the memset, plus a regression fixture test) is sound. Severity MEDIUM is appropriate: test-infrastructure latent corruption, currently masked, affecting any fixture sequence that uses (0,0).

REFUTED: The mechanical trace is accurate (bb_fixtures.h:50 memset -> location=0=BB_LOC_ON_PITCH per bb_types.h:50, x=y=0 -> bb_match.c:52-54 clears grid[0][0] before the new placement at :58), but under the reachability lens the finding fails as a MEDIUM bug. (1) bb_fixtures.h is test-only: it is included by 8 test files plus 3 offline tools and is never compiled into the engine library, the RL binding, or the fuzz harness — fuzz initializes via bb_match_init (engine/tests/fuzz_match.c:27) and the PufferLib env via bb_match_init_random (puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:351), so no real match, training run, or fuzzed input can ever execute fx_player. All 18 production bb_place call sites (proc_move.c, proc_block.c, proc_match.c, proc_ttm.c) operate on players whose location/x/y are genuine, so the stale-square cleanup there is always correct. (2) Exhaustive search finds exactly ONE (0,0) fixture placement in the entire tree: test_rules_movement.c:137, the claim's own example. The tools that use fixtures never place at (0,0) (dist_dump.c coords: 10,5 / 11,5 / 2,2 / 23,12 / 20,10 / 8,5 / 9,5; gen_goldens.c and coverage_report.c use only fx_pick_smart, which places nothing). (3) At that single site the corruption is provably outcome-neutral: begin_move (test_rules_movement.c:32-38) asserts BB_STATUS_DECISION at line 143 and passes, empirically proving activation/declare legality does not read grid[0][0]; count_steps (lines 40-48) only reads occupancy at the destination squares (1,0),(0,1),(1,1), which are empty in both corrupt and clean states — grid[0][0] is never consulted, so the test verifies exactly the off-pitch property it documents with identical semantics either way. The claimed escalation ('any opponent could legally step onto the empty (0,0)') is unreachable inside the test: the lone opponent at (24,14) is never activated and the test ends immediately after count_steps; and a single mover step would self-heal the grid via proc_move.c:63. The claim itself concedes the test passes green and frames the harm as hypothetical future assertions. A test-fixture hygiene wart with one outcome-neutral trigger and zero production/fuzz/training reachability is at most a low-priority hardening suggestion, not a MEDIUM board-state-corruption bug.

UPHELD: Claim survives the design-intent attack; every link in the trace verified against the code. (1) bb_fixtures.h:50 memset zeroes the player struct, and bb_types.h:50 makes BB_LOC_ON_PITCH = 0, so the slot reads as on-pitch at (0,0); (2) bb_place (bb_match.c:50-58) then unconditionally executes the stale-square cleanup `if (p->location == BB_LOC_ON_PITCH) m->grid[p->x][p->y] = 0;` clearing grid[0][0] on every fx_player call before the new placement at line 58. (3) The live trigger is verbatim real: test_rules_movement.c:137 places the mover at (0,0) (grid[0][0]=1) and line 138's fx_lineman(&m,1,9,24,14) wipes that entry — confirmed divergent state (player struct ON_PITCH at (0,0), grid empty). (4) Nothing repairs it: turn_start (proc_turn.c:9-33) clears per-turn flags only and never resyncs the grid; can_activate and the count_steps destinations (1,0)/(0,1)/(1,1) never consult grid[0][0], which is exactly why the test stays green as the claim itself states. (5) Design-intent yields no refutation: DECISIONS.md D1-D24 say nothing about fixtures or bb_place; no comment documents a precondition; and fx_match_midturn's deliberate BB_LOC_ABSENT loop (bb_fixtures.h:25-27) shows the fixture author's intent was precisely that memset-zeroed slots NOT read as on-pitch at (0,0) — fx_player's memset undoes that, so the current behavior contradicts the documented-by-code intent rather than embodying it. The proposed fix is test-only, leaves engine bb_place semantics untouched, and breaks no documented invariant. Only cosmetic defects: memset is at line 50 not 51, and MEDIUM is arguably generous for a test-fixture-only latent bug, but the claim is explicit that the test currently passes. A grep confirms test_rules_movement.c:137 is the sole (0,0) placement site, matching the claim's scoping.

title: build_dist_dump prefers stale build/main/obj over build/obj — layer-2 conformance currently validates an outdated engine
claim: build_dist_dump picks the first existing object directory from ("build/main/obj", "build/obj") with no freshness comparison between the two candidates. Nothing in the repo rebuilds build/main (Makefile's BUILD defaults to `build`, so `make` only updates build/obj; the only references to build/main are a comment in dist_dump.c:37 and validation/README.md:119). Right now the two trees demonstrably diverge: build/main/obj/bb_procgen.o (19136 bytes, mtime Jun 3 07:07:59) differs from build/obj/bb_procgen.o (19920 bytes, mtime Jun 3 07:53:13) — `cmp` confirms different contents, i.e. bb_procgen (the procedural team generator feeding training) changed after build/main was last built. The next `python3 validation/conformance.py` run will rebuild dist_dump against the stale 07:07 engine (the staleness check at lines 186-189 only compares the binary's mtime against the CHOSEN dir's objects), so dice-conformance pass/fail verdicts apply to an engine that no longer matches the tree being trained on.
evidence: conformance.py:176-181: `for cand in ("build/main/obj", "build/obj"): ... objs_dir = d; break` — first-hit wins. Verified on disk: `cmp build/obj/bb_procgen.o build/main/obj/bb_procgen.o` -> DIFFERS (19136 vs 19920 bytes; build/main objs mtime 07:07:59 < build/obj 07:53:13). `grep -rn 'build/main'` across the repo finds no producer, only dist_dump.c:37 (comment), validation/README.md:119, and this selection loop, so build/main can only go staler. dist_dump binary (build/dist_dump, 07:05:05) is older than build/main's objects, so the rebuild WILL trigger — against the stale set.
fix: Select the candidate directory whose newest .o is most recent (or drop "build/main/obj" entirely since the Makefile only ever writes build/obj), and include tools/../engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h (included by dist_dump.c:45) in the newest_dep mtime computation at lines 186-188 so fixture edits also force a relink.
votes: UPHELD: Claim survives full adversarial verification — every factual element checks out under code-path and on-disk tracing. (1) conformance.py:176-180 is genuinely first-hit-wins over ("build/main/obj", "build/obj") with no cross-candidate freshness comparison; build/main/obj exists with 22 .o files so it always wins. (2) Makefile:10 `BUILD ?= build` means plain `make` only refreshes build/obj; repo-wide grep confirms the only build/main references are dist_dump.c:37 (comment), validation/README.md:119, and the selection loop — no producer, and neither DECISIONS.md, CLAUDE.md, nor the bb-validation skill documents build/main as a deliberate workflow, so this is not by-design. (3) The divergence is real and semantically meaningful: cmp confirms build/main/obj/bb_procgen.o (19136 B, Jun 3 07:07) differs from build/obj/bb_procgen.o (19920 B, 07:53); git shows commit 1533627 (07:54:32, 'Fix Codex review HIGHs... 12 skill slots') added the PG_MAX_SKILLS=12 advancement cap to bb_procgen.c — i.e., build/main lacks one of the six HIGH fixes from the prior deep review. (4) The rebuild WILL link the stale set: DIST_DUMP_BIN (build/dist_dump, 07:05:05) is older than build/main/obj's newest object (07:08), so newest_dep at lines 186-188 forces a relink against build/main/obj. (5) The proposed fix's bb_fixtures.h point also verifies: dist_dump.c:45 includes ../engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h and it is absent from newest_dep. One mitigating nuance that does NOT refute: today's sole divergent object exports only bb_match_init_random, which dist_dump never calls (random_matches at dist_dump.c:348 uses bb_match_init with fixed rosters), so the current instant's chi-square verdicts are numerically unaffected — but the mechanism guarantees monotonic staleness for all 22 engine objects, so the next dice-path source change rebuilt via plain `make` would have conformance silently validating an old engine. MEDIUM severity is appropriate; the bug and fix direction are correct.

REFUTED: The claim's mechanics are accurate but its impact is unreachable, and the impact IS the claim ("layer-2 conformance currently validates an outdated engine"). Verified facts: (1) conformance.py:176-180 does prefer build/main/obj first-hit, nothing rebuilds build/main (Makefile:10 BUILD?=build), and the next run will relink dist_dump against the 07:07 objects (build/dist_dump mtime 07:05:05 < 07:07). (2) BUT a byte-level cmp of all 21 .o files between build/obj and build/main/obj shows exactly ONE divergent object: bb_procgen.o. Every other object — bb_match.o, proc_block.o, proc_move.o, proc_ball.o, bb_skills.o, bb_hooks.o, etc. (the entirety of dist_dump's executable surface) — is byte-identical; the 07:53 make only refreshed mtimes. (3) bb_procgen.o exports a single function, _bb_match_init_random (nm: one T symbol + local _procgen_squad), and it is dead code in the dist_dump link: nm -u across every other engine object finds zero references; tools/dist_dump.c and engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h never reference procgen; dist_dump creates matches via bb_match_init (dist_dump.c:348), which lives in byte-identical bb_match.o. (4) No constructor escape: otool -l on bb_procgen.o shows only __text + debug/unwind sections, no __mod_init_func — the stale code cannot execute even at process startup (checked specifically because dist_dump.c:34-36 links objects to preserve constructor-registered skill hooks; those constructors live in other, byte-identical objects). (5) Therefore the dist_dump binary linked from build/main/obj is behaviorally identical to one linked from build/obj — every conformance verdict it produces applies bit-for-bit to the current engine. (6) The 'tree being trained on' framing also fails: training never consumes build/*/obj — the PufferLib binding compiles engine sources directly via symlinks (puffer/bloodbowl/engine -> ../../engine/src, puffer/bloodbowl/bb -> ../../engine/include/bb), and bb_match_init_random's only callers are engine/tests/test_match.c:146,165 (built fresh from build/obj into build/bb_tests) and puffer/bloodbowl/bloodbowl.h:358 (built from sources). Procgen is structurally outside layer 2's measured surface regardless of which object dir is chosen — dist_dump.c's scenarios (blocks, armour, injury, casualty, dodge, rush, fixture-driven random matches) cannot reach it. Residual truth worth a one-line note, not this finding: the first-hit selection is fragile going forward — if a REACHABLE object (e.g., proc_block.o) ever diverged while build/main sat frozen, verdicts would genuinely go stale, and the lines 186-189 staleness check (binary mtime vs chosen dir only) would never force a relink after the first one. But as stated and evidenced (present tense, sole evidence being the bb_procgen.o divergence), the claim of current mis-validation is disproven: the only divergent code is unreachable from any dist_dump execution path.

UPHELD: Claim survives the design-intent attack and is independently confirmed — verification strengthened it. (1) No documented intent shields the code: DECISIONS.md D1-D24 never mentions build/main or conformance build-dir selection; project CLAUDE.md and the bb-validation skill contain no build/main references; the only traces (tools/dist_dump.c:37 comment, validation/README.md:119) are a copied manual link line with no rationale — a historical artifact, not an invariant. The proposed freshest-candidate fix breaks nothing documented. (2) Every evidence element verified by direct read/inspection: conformance.py:176-180 is first-hit-wins; the staleness check at 186-189 only considers the chosen dir; Makefile:10 BUILD?=build means plain make only refreshes build/obj (only other override is build/asan at Makefile:50); build/main/obj exists and wins; cmp confirms bb_procgen.o differs (19136@07:07 vs 19920@07:53); build/dist_dump (07:05) is older than build/main's newest object (proc_turn.o 07:08) so the next conformance run WILL relink against the stale set. (3) Smoking gun the reviewer missed: diffing ALL objects between the trees shows bb_procgen.o is the ONLY divergent one (ruling out flag noise), and git log shows the divergence is commit 1533627 (Jun 3 07:54:32) 'Fix Codex review HIGHs: ... 12 skill slots' touching engine/src/bb_procgen.c — i.e., build/main is stale by exactly the prior deep review's HIGH-severity fixes, so the next layer-2 conformance verdict would validate the pre-fix engine. The secondary fix point also holds: dist_dump.c includes ../engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h, which is absent from the newest_dep mtime computation at conformance.py:186-188. One caveat for the fixer: something out-of-band does build build/main (make BUILD=build/main, consistent with the build/{ball,blk,inj,...} isolation pattern), so prefer the freshness-comparison fix over dropping the candidate outright.

title: Test binary has no dependency on bb_fixtures.h — fixture-only edits run a stale bb_tests and report green
claim: The $(TESTBIN) rule lists $(TEST_SRC), bb_test.h, bb_test_main.c and $(OBJ) as prerequisites but omits engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h, and the test compile line has no -MMD dependency generation (only engine/src objects get .d files via line 28/30). bb_fixtures.h is included by 8 of the test translation units (test_match.c, test_rules_{ball,injury,movement,blocking,structure}.c, test_skills_*.c) and is not included by any engine/src/*.c, so editing ONLY bb_fixtures.h triggers no $(OBJ) rebuild and no $(TESTBIN) relink: `make test` runs the previous binary compiled with the old fixture code and prints all-green. This directly bites the fix for the fx_player grid-corruption finding: patch bb_fixtures.h, run `make test`, and the unmodified stale binary 'confirms' nothing changed. Engine headers under engine/include are safe only indirectly (they rebuild $(OBJ) via the .d files, which then makes $(TESTBIN) out of date); the tests-local fixtures header is the one gap.
evidence: Makefile:43 `$(TESTBIN): $(TEST_SRC) engine/tests/bb_test.h engine/tests/bb_test_main.c $(OBJ)` — bb_fixtures.h absent; Makefile:44 compiles tests with `$(CC) $(CFLAGS) -Iengine/tests ...` (no -MMD, no .d include for these objects; line 30's `-include $(OBJ:.o=.d)` covers only $(BUILD)/obj/*.d from engine/src). `grep -l bb_fixtures.h engine/tests/*.c` -> 8 test files; `grep bb_fixtures engine/src/*.c` -> none, so no transitive trigger exists. After `touch engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h`, every prerequisite of $(TESTBIN) is older than the binary, so make declares it up to date.
fix: Add engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h to the $(TESTBIN) prerequisite list (one word on line 43), or compile tests with -MMD -MP into $(BUILD) and -include their .d files.
votes: UPHELD: Claim CONFIRMED by exhaustive static verification plus a conclusive read-only GNU make dry-run. (1) Makefile:43 lists $(TEST_SRC), engine/tests/bb_test.h, engine/tests/bb_test_main.c, $(OBJ) as $(TESTBIN) prerequisites — bb_fixtures.h is absent. (2) Makefile:44 compiles all tests directly to the binary with no -MMD; the only depfile generation is the engine/src pattern rule (Makefile:28), and Makefile:30 includes only $(OBJ:.o=.d). Grep of every .d under build/ finds zero references to bb_fixtures, so no transitive trigger exists. (3) Exactly 8 test TUs include bb_fixtures.h (e.g. engine/tests/test_match.c:6); no engine/src/*.c, engine/include file, bb_test.h, or bb_test_main.c includes it, so no listed prerequisite goes out of date when it changes. (4) Dispositive proof without modifying anything: `make -n -W engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h test` (pretend-modified, dry-run) outputs only `./build/bb_tests` — no recompile, no relink; the stale binary runs and reports green. Control `make -n -W engine/tests/bb_test.h test` triggers the full recompile+relink, confirming the gap is specific to bb_fixtures.h. (5) The asan target (Makefile:49-50) recursively uses the same rule and inherits the gap; no alternate build system (no CMake, no nested Makefile) covers it. The proposed fix (add engine/tests/bb_fixtures.h to line 43, or use -MMD for test objects) is correct. Not documented as intentional in DECISIONS.md or CLAUDE.md.
